proposals.title: Structural Dynamics and Thermostability of the VAC8-VAC12 Membrane Motor Complex in Vacuolar Function
proposals.motivation: Vacuolar inheritance in yeast requires coordinated membrane dynamics, and the VAC8 and VAC12 complementation groups are essential for this process, yet their molecular function remains unclear. Pre-2026 work on cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) and autophagy pathways has identified key proteins and mutants, but the physical nature of the putative motor complex driving vacuole movement is undefined. Understanding the structural basis of this complex is critical for elucidating fundamental mechanisms of organelle inheritance and membrane trafficking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that VAC8 and VAC12 proteins form a membrane-associated motor complex whose function depends not on static rigidity but on specific, localized structural flexibility. We propose that this inherent flexibility is a key determinant of the complex's function and its resilience to thermal stress.
proposals.method: We will first purify the native VAC8-VAC12 complex from yeast using tandem affinity purification. We will then employ hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) to map regions of dynamic flexibility under physiological and elevated temperatures. Concurrently, we will use single-molecule FRET (smFRET) with site-specifically labeled complex subunits reconstituted into proteoliposomes to measure real-time conformational changes during ATP hydrolysis. Thermostability will be assessed by monitoring complex integrity and ATPase activity after heat challenge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first biochemical and structural characterization of the VAC8-VAC12 complex, defining it as a bona fide membrane motor. It will challenge the simplistic paradigm linking thermostability solely to rigidity by demonstrating how functional flexibility is maintained under stress. The findings will directly inform models of vacuolar inheritance and related processes like autophagy.
proposals.novelty: While VAC8 and VAC12 are known genetic factors, their direct interaction and biochemical function as a complex is unexplored. Applying HDX-MS and smFRET to measure flexibility in a native membrane motor system, rather than a soluble enzyme, provides a novel test of flexibility-stability relationships in a biologically relevant context.

proposals.title: Defining a causal link between V-ATPase c-ring stoichiometry and inheritance pathology via engineered vacuole mis-segregation
proposals.motivation: Organelle inheritance is critical for cellular fitness, yet the specific biophysical properties of an organelle that dictate the severity of mis-segregation defects remain poorly defined. The V-ATPase, a key regulator of vacuolar pH, exhibits variable c-ring stoichiometry, which is known to alter its proton-pumping efficiency per rotation. This presents a unique, fixed structural parameter that could directly influence the organelle's capacity to maintain homeostasis when inherited incorrectly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fixed proton-pumping capacity dictated by V-ATPase c-ring stoichiometry is a primary determinant of the cellular stress caused by vacuole mis-segregation, such that vacuoles harboring pumps with lower intrinsic efficiency will cause more severe pH dysregulation and metabolic defects in daughter cells.
proposals.method: We will utilize yeast strains with defined V-ATPase c-ring stoichiometries (e.g., vma3Δ strains complemented with alleles encoding 10- or 14-subunit rings). Using established cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway tools, we will engineer the mis-targeting of specific cargo (e.g., Ape1) to create distinct, identifiable vacuole populations. These vacuoles will then be actively mis-segregated during mitosis using anchored protein systems (e.g., Hook-FKBP/FRB) to direct them to the bud. We will quantitatively assess consequences via ratiometric pHluorin imaging, monitoring of ion-sensitive fluorescent dyes, and assays for metabolic stress (e.g., ROS detection, growth assays).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between a fundamental molecular architecture (c-ring stoichiometry) and the phenotypic outcome of an organelle inheritance error. It will move the field beyond correlative observations by providing a tunable, mechanistic driver for precision inheritance studies. Furthermore, it will reveal how static structural variations in organellar machinery can predispose cells to stress upon division errors.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in treating the V-ATPase c-ring not as a passive structural feature but as an active, tunable input to control the severity of an inheritance defect, fusing precise biochemical engineering with active organelle mis-targeting tools in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Amphiphilic Modulators of Membrane Protein Sorting Kinetics in Vacuole Inheritance via the Vps Pathway
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in yeast is a canonical model for organelle biogenesis, governed by protein-protein interactions in pathways like Vps. However, the role of the membrane's physicochemical environment in sorting kinetics is poorly understood. Pre-2026 work on alternative protein sorting pathways and cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting suggests complex regulation, yet the potential for endogenous lipids or exogenous amphiphiles to actively direct this process remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Detergent-like amphiphiles (e.g., LDAO) and specific endogenous lipids are not inert but act as active modulators that create a permissive chemical environment, accelerating the partitioning and sorting kinetics of vacuolar membrane proteins (e.g., Vph1p, Vma proteins) into the inheritance machinery during cell division.
proposals.method: We will employ a quantitative, fluorescence microscopy-based assay to track the kinetics of GFP-tagged vacuolar membrane proteins (Vph1, Vma5) during inheritance in wild-type and vps mutant strains. Using in vitro reconstitution with purified vacuoles and inheritance intermediates, we will systematically titrate defined amphiphiles (LDAO, lyso-lipids) and measure sorting efficiency via immunoblot and protease protection assays. Kinetic parameters will be derived from time-course experiments, comparing conditions with and without amphiphile addition to dissect their specific effect on Vps-dependent sorting.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel physicochemical regulatory layer in organelle inheritance, moving beyond a purely protein-centric view. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how membrane composition influences sorting kinetics, potentially explaining variability in inheritance fidelity and offering new interpretations for classical biochemical data using detergents.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the long-standing assumption of detergent inertness in cell biological assays and probes an overlooked dimension of regulation—where the chemical properties of amphiphiles, not just protein signals, actively govern sorting dynamics.

proposals.title: A Multi-Parametric Kinetic Screen Identifies Genetic Modulators of V-ATPase Mechanochemistry and Lipid Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: The vacuolar ATPase (V-ATPase) is essential for organelle acidification, nutrient sensing, and autophagy, processes central to Klionsky's work on vacuolar function and cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting. While genetic screens have identified mutants with defective V-ATPase assembly or proton transport, the specific molecular steps in its rotary mechanochemistry and its regulation by the membrane lipid environment remain poorly understood. A systematic approach is needed to dissect how individual gene products contribute to distinct kinetic transitions and lipid interactions of the motor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic variation of the mild detergent LDAO, which perturbs lipid-protein interactions, will reveal distinct classes of V-ATPase mutants that specifically alter motor kinetics (dwell times, stepping rates) rather than simply abolishing function, thereby linking specific genes to discrete mechanical or conformational steps within the complex.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide screen in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, quantifying V-ATPase single-molecule kinetics for each viable deletion mutant using a pre-2026 established optical trapping assay with bead-labeled V1 subunits. Each mutant strain will be assayed across a titrated range of LDAO concentrations (0-0.03%). For each condition, we will measure rotational dwell time distributions and stepping rates to generate a multi-dimensional phenotypic signature. Mutants will be classified using principal component analysis and hierarchical clustering based on their kinetic parameters and LDAO sensitivity profiles, pinpointing genes affecting specific mechanistic transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond binary functional classification to establish a detailed kinetic taxonomy of V-ATPase mutants, directly linking gene function to specific mechanical steps. It will identify novel genetic factors involved in lipid sensing and conformational coupling within the complex, providing a mechanistic framework for understanding how vacuolar acidification is regulated in pathways like autophagy.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel because it uses a kinetic perturbagen (LDAO) in a quantitative, multi-parameter screen to dissect motor mechanism genetically, whereas previous screens primarily assessed endpoint phenotypes like growth or bulk organelle acidification.

proposals.title: Temperature-Dependent Modulation of Vacuole Inheritance Fidelity by Vps Protein Dynamics in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in yeast is a critical, Vps-mediated process ensuring organelle partitioning during cell division. Environmental temperature fluctuations are a common stressor, yet the molecular mechanisms by which the fidelity of this essential process is maintained or compromised across a physiological temperature range remain unknown. Understanding how temperature affects the protein machinery of organelle inheritance bridges the fields of cell biology and biophysics, addressing a fundamental gap in our knowledge of cellular adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that temperature-induced changes in the structural flexibility of key Vps sorting and vacuole inheritance proteins (e.g., Vps1, Vps34, Vac8) create or disrupt critical functional interfaces, thereby directly modulating the efficiency of vacuole inheritance.
proposals.method: We will employ hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) on purified target proteins and complexes to experimentally map temperature-dependent flexibility changes. These data will guide and validate all-atom molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS) to identify dynamic 'hotspots' at specific interfaces. Functional consequences will be assayed in yeast using established microscopy-based vacuole inheritance assays in wild-type and relevant mutants (e.g., vpsΔ) across a 20-37°C range, correlating inheritance efficiency with biophysical dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, mechanistic link between the intrinsic biophysical properties of Vps pathway proteins and the robustness of an essential organelle inheritance process. It will identify specific protein domains or interfaces whose thermal stability is critical for faithful partitioning, providing a new paradigm for understanding how cells adapt core machinery to environmental stress.
proposals.novelty: While vacuole inheritance and Vps pathways are well-studied, the explicit integration of protein flexibility dynamics from biophysical techniques with functional organelle biology to explain temperature-dependent phenotypic outcomes is a novel and non-obvious approach.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule FRET Reveals Thermal Sensitivity of Vacuole Inheritance Machinery in Arabidopsis
proposals.motivation: Plant vacuoles are essential for growth, stress response, and cellular homeostasis, yet the molecular mechanisms governing their faithful inheritance during cell division remain poorly understood. Climate change increases the frequency of heat stress events, which can disrupt fundamental cellular processes like organelle biogenesis and partitioning. This project directly addresses how a critical housekeeping process—vacuole inheritance—is affected by thermal stress at the molecular level, linking cell biology to abiotic stress resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the conformational dynamics and assembly of plant-specific vacuole inheritance complexes, such as those involving the Vacuole Inheritance Protein 1 (VIP1) and SNARE proteins, are thermally sensitive, and that specific failure modes in these dynamics under heat stress lead to defective vacuole partitioning.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule FRET (smFRET) to monitor real-time conformational changes in key proteins during in vitro reconstitution of vacuole membrane fission events. Specifically, Arabidopsis thaliana VIP1 and the tonoplast SNARE VTI11 will be site-specifically labeled with donor (Cy3) and acceptor (Cy5) fluorophores. Using a custom microfluidic temperature stage coupled to a total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscope, we will acquire smFRET trajectories of these proteins interacting with liposomes mimicking the tonoplast under controlled thermal stress (22°C to 42°C). Data will be analyzed using hidden Markov modeling (HMM) to identify discrete conformational states and transition rates, quantifying thermal perturbation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, dynamic measurement of the plant vacuole inheritance machinery in action, defining its intrinsic thermal limits. It will identify specific molecular steps (e.g., SNARE complex zippering, VIP1 oligomerization) that are most vulnerable to heat, offering a mechanistic explanation for observed inheritance defects in heat-sensitive mutants. This bridges the gap between genetic studies of vacuolar biogenesis and the physical principles of organelle logistics under stress.
proposals.novelty: While smFRET has been used to study protein dynamics and yeast autophagy (e.g., Atg proteins), its application to monitor plant-specific organelle inheritance complexes under precisely controlled thermal stress is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond static localization or genetic necessity to reveal the real-time biophysical failure modes of a critical cellular process.

proposals.title: Direct Membrane Partitioning Mediates Vacuole Inheritance in Saccharomyces cerevisiae: A Mechanism Analogous to Bacterial Plasmid Segregation
proposals.motivation: Organelle inheritance is fundamental for cellular viability, yet the precise mechanism for vacuole inheritance in yeast remains unresolved. Pre-2026 models emphasize vesicular carriers from the mother vacuole or de novo biogenesis, but a direct, active segregation of pre-existing membrane, analogous to bacterial plasmid partitioning, has not been rigorously tested. Resolving this is critical for understanding organelle homeostasis and the fidelity of cellular reproduction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pre-existing, labeled vacuolar membrane is actively and directly partitioned into the bud during cytokinesis via a non-vesicular mechanism, independent of new membrane biogenesis and canonical vesicular trafficking pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a pulse-chase strategy using a photoactivatable GFP fused to the vacuolar membrane protein Vph1p. Following photoactivation in mother cells, we will inhibit new membrane synthesis (e.g., using cerulenin) and vesicular trafficking (using sec mutants or chemical inhibitors). Using time-lapse fluorescence microscopy, we will quantitatively track the movement of the pre-labeled membrane into emerging buds during cell division. Key quantitative metrics will include fluorescence intensity ratios between mother and bud over time and the kinetics of partitioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive, quantitative framework to distinguish between de novo biogenesis, vesicular transfer, and direct partitioning models of vacuole inheritance. It will identify the cellular machinery required for direct segregation, potentially revealing novel cytoskeletal or tethering factors. The findings will provide a paradigm for the inheritance of other membrane-bound organelles.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges the assumption that vesicular carriers are obligatory, introducing a non-vesicular, plasmid-like segregation model. It uniquely combines precise membrane labeling with targeted pathway inhibition to isolate and observe a putative direct partitioning mechanism previously obscured by concurrent biosynthetic pathways.

proposals.title: Direct Mother-to-Daughter Vacuole Partitioning is the Primary Mechanism of Vacuole Inheritance in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Organelle inheritance is fundamental for cellular homeostasis, yet the mechanism for vacuole inheritance remains unresolved. Pre-2026 work has identified mutants defective in vacuole inheritance and characterized protein targeting pathways to the vacuole, but it is unclear if inheritance occurs via direct partitioning of the maternal organelle, vesicular carriers, or de novo biogenesis. Resolving this is critical for understanding organelle biogenesis and cellular memory of organelle identity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vacuole inheritance in budding yeast occurs primarily through the direct, cytoskeleton-mediated transfer of a membrane compartment from the mother vacuole to the bud, rather than through vesicular carriers originating from the secretory pathway or purely de novo synthesis.
proposals.method: We will employ live-cell imaging of fluorescently tagged vacuolar membrane proteins (e.g., Vph1-GFP) and luminal markers in wild-type and inheritance mutants (e.g., vac mutants) to track organelle dynamics. Using photobleaching techniques (FRAP/FLIP), we will assess membrane continuity between mother and bud vacuoles. Genetic approaches will disrupt vesicular trafficking (e.g., sec mutants) and the cytoplasm-to-vacuole (Cvt) pathway to test their contribution, while biochemical fractionation will isolate and characterize any putative inheritance-specific vesicles.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the primary physical mechanism of vacuole inheritance, distinguishing between competing models. It will integrate the field's understanding of vacuolar protein sorting pathways with the process of organelle segregation, revealing how cellular memory of organelle identity is maintained during division.
proposals.novelty: While vacuole inheritance mutants are known, direct testing of membrane continuity versus vesicular carrier models using modern live-cell imaging and photobleaching in the context of defined trafficking mutants is lacking. This proposal directly challenges the assumption that vesicular transport is required for inheritance.

proposals.title: Osmotic Pressure as a Primary Driver of Vacuole Inheritance: A Self-Organizing System in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in budding yeast is a critical, non-random process essential for cellular homeostasis. The prevailing model posits that the cytoskeleton, particularly the class V myosin motor Myo2p, actively pulls the vacuole into the daughter bud. However, this model may be incomplete, as it does not fully account for the physical forces generated by the organelle itself. Drawing an analogy from plant cell biology, where turgor pressure is the primary engine for growth, we propose a paradigm shift in understanding organelle partitioning.
proposals.hypothesis: Vacuole inheritance is fundamentally a pressure-driven, self-organizing process where osmotic gradients generate hydrostatic force for movement and fission, while the cytoskeleton acts primarily as a guiding and stabilizing scaffold rather than the sole motor.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of genetic, cell biological, and biophysical approaches. First, using mutants defective in cytoskeletal motors (myo2, vac8) and osmotic regulators (e.g., ena1Δ nha1Δ), we will quantify vacuole inheritance fidelity under manipulated osmotic conditions. Second, we will directly measure intravacuolar pressure dynamics during budding using a calibrated, vacuole-targeted FRET-based nanosensor (akin to pre-2026 FLIPPi sensors for chloride). Third, we will use high-resolution live-cell imaging to track vacuole morphology and inheritance in real-time while acutely perturbing osmotic gradients with sorbitol or hypo-osmotic shocks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new physical framework for organelle inheritance, moving beyond a purely cytoskeleton-centric view. It will provide the first direct measurements of intravacuolar pressure in yeast and identify the minimal physical requirements for vacuole partitioning. This could reveal novel, conserved principles of cellular organization driven by osmotic forces.
proposals.novelty: The proposal challenges the canonical 'motor-pulling' model by introducing osmotic pressure as a primary force generator, a concept well-established in plant cells but largely unexplored for organelle dynamics in yeast. It reframes the cytoskeleton's role from driver to guide.

proposals.title: Defining the VAC8-VAC12 Functional Module: An Integrative Analysis of Its Role in Vacuolar Membrane Trafficking and Stress Response
proposals.motivation: Vacuolar protein sorting and membrane dynamics are critical for cellular homeostasis, yet many molecular players function in poorly defined complexes. VAC8 and VAC12 are both implicated in vacuolar inheritance and morphology, but their relationship and precise mechanistic roles remain unclear. A module-based analysis is essential to move beyond single-gene studies and understand how these proteins coordinate trafficking pathways, particularly under stress conditions that challenge vacuolar integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that VAC8 and VAC12 form a core functional module that acts as a regulatory hub for specific vacuolar membrane trafficking pathways, and that acute disruption of this module will reveal distinct, pathway-specific defects not apparent in chronic mutants.
proposals.method: We will endogenously tag VAC8 and VAC12 with fluorescent proteins (GFP, mCherry) and affinity tags (3xFLAG, BioID2) in yeast. Proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) will map interactomes during normal growth and under osmotic/nutrient stress. Auxin-inducible degron (AID) tags will enable rapid, conditional protein depletion to assay acute phenotypes in trafficking assays (e.g., FM4-64 uptake, ALP maturation). Native complexes will be purified via FLAG-immunoprecipitation for analysis by mass spectrometry and negative-stain electron microscopy to define stoichiometry and architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the VAC8-VAC12 complex as a defined functional unit, directly linking its cellular role to its biochemical composition. It will identify novel stress-specific interactors and provide a mechanistic framework for how this module governs specific trafficking routes, potentially related to cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting or vacuole inheritance pathways.
proposals.novelty: While VAC8 and VAC12 have been studied individually, this project uniquely integrates acute degradation, dynamic proximity labeling, and native complex purification to define them as a coherent module, moving from genetic interaction to a direct biochemical and functional entity.

proposals.title: Deorphanization of the VAC8-VAC12 Gene Products: A Systematic Approach to Define Their Binding Partners and Functions in Vacuolar Biogenesis
proposals.motivation: The VAC8-VAC12 genes were identified in classical yeast genetic screens for vacuolar protein sorting (vps) mutants, but their encoded proteins remain molecularly and functionally uncharacterized, representing a significant gap in our understanding of vacuole biogenesis. This organelle is crucial for autophagy, nutrient storage, and cellular homeostasis, and defective vacuolar pathways are linked to disease. A systematic characterization of these 'orphan' vacuolar proteins is therefore essential to complete the molecular map of vacuolar assembly and function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VAC8-VAC12 proteins are novel components of the vacuolar membrane or associated machinery, and that systematic identification of their binding partners and structural features will reveal their specific roles in vacuolar biogenesis, protein targeting, or organelle inheritance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the pharmacological deorphanization pipeline. First, we will heterologously express and purify the VAC8-VAC12 proteins, tagging them for pull-downs and microscopy. Second, we will perform high-throughput yeast two-hybrid and affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS) screens against comprehensive yeast genomic libraries to identify interacting proteins and potential small molecule ligands. Third, functional validation will use established assays for vacuolar morphology (FM4-64 staining), protease sorting (CPY secretion assays), and autophagy (GFP-Atg8 processing). Finally, we will employ X-ray crystallography or cryo-EM on promising targets to determine atomic structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly assign biochemical functions and interaction networks to the enigmatic VAC8-VAC12 proteins, moving them from genetic loci to defined molecular players. It will potentially identify new subunits of known complexes or reveal entirely novel pathways in vacuolar biology, providing mechanistic insights that could explain the defective phenotypes of the original mutants.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the structured, high-throughput 'deorphanization' concept from receptor pharmacology to the specific problem of uncharacterized gene products in organelle biology, creating a systematic pipeline to bridge the gap between genetic identification and biochemical mechanism for the VAC8-VAC12 proteins.

proposals.title: Direct Measurement of Torque and Coupling Efficiency in the Yeast Vacuolar ATPase Vo Sector Using Magnetic Tweezers
proposals.motivation: The vacuolar ATPase (V-ATPase) is essential for organelle acidification and cellular homeostasis, yet the mechanochemical coupling mechanism of its membrane-embedded, proton-translocating Vo sector remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies have identified VAC8 and VAC12 as putative Vo components in yeast, with mutants showing defective vacuolar protein sorting and acidification, but their precise roles in force generation are unknown. Establishing a direct structure-mechanism relationship for this Fo-like rotary motor is a critical gap in our understanding of organellar bioenergetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific VAC gene products (VAC8 and VAC12) are essential for efficient mechanochemical coupling within the Vo sector, and that mutations in these components will directly alter the stall torque and rotational kinetics of the motor, leading to measurable mechanical defects such as increased slippage.
proposals.method: We will purify wild-type and mutant Vo complexes (e.g., vac8Δ, vac12Δ) from yeast strains and reconstitute them into proteoliposomes. Individual proteoliposomes containing Vo will be tethered to a functionalized coverslip and to a superparamagnetic bead. Using a custom magnetic tweezers setup, we will apply precisely controlled external torque to the bead and measure the rotational displacement and stall force of the Vo motor in response to an imposed proton-motive force. This allows direct quantification of parameters like stall torque, rotational step size, and coupling efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurements of torque generation and coupling efficiency for the V-ATPase Vo sector. It will assign specific mechanical functions—such as torque generation, slippage, or structural coordination—to the VAC8 and VAC12 gene products, moving beyond correlative phenotypic analysis. This establishes a foundational biomechanical framework for understanding V-ATPase function and its dysfunction in disease.
proposals.novelty: While magnetic tweezers have been used to study bacterial FoF1-ATPase, their application to the eukaryotic V-ATPase, specifically to dissect the mechanical role of non-canonical subunits like VAC8/VAC12 identified through genetic screens, is unprecedented. This directly merges yeast genetics/vacuolar protein sorting pathways with single-molecule biomechanics.

proposals.title: A Genome-Wide Screen Identifies the Core Machinery and Pathway for Intervacuole Communication During Yeast Cytokinesis
proposals.motivation: The yeast vacuole is a dynamic organelle that must be faithfully inherited during cell division, requiring communication between mother and daughter vacuoles. While VAC8, VAC9, and VAC12 are known to be involved, the complete molecular machinery and the mechanistic pathway for this essential organellar communication remain undefined. An unbiased, systematic approach is needed to move beyond these candidate genes and establish a comprehensive model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, genetically defined set of proteins beyond the known VAC genes forms a functional pathway to mediate the physical and signaling communication required for vacuole inheritance.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide visual screen in S. cerevisiae using a GFP-tagged vacuolar membrane marker (Vph1-GFP) to identify mutants defective in vacuole inheritance or morphology during budding. Hits will be validated and ordered into a functional pathway using genetic epistasis analysis. The physical interactions of core machinery components will be determined by co-immunoprecipitation and yeast two-hybrid assays, while their dynamic localization and requisite activities (e.g., tethering, lipid transfer) will be characterized using high-resolution, time-lapse fluorescence microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first complete genetic inventory of genes required for intervacuole communication, establishing a validated functional pathway. It will mechanistically define the protein complexes that execute this process, significantly advancing our understanding of organelle inheritance and inter-organelle communication in eukaryotic cells.
proposals.novelty: This project moves beyond the presupposed, limited role of VAC8/9/12 by using an unbiased genetic screen to identify the full complement of machinery, a strategy not previously applied comprehensively to this specific communication problem.

proposals.title: Vacuolar Gap Junctions: A Role for Vacuole Inheritance Proteins in Forming a Selective Intervacuole Channel in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: In yeast, vacuole inheritance during cell division requires coordinated communication and content transfer between mother and daughter vacuoles, yet the molecular machinery enabling direct intervacuole communication remains unknown. This gap limits our understanding of organelle homeostasis and inheritance, processes central to cell biology and relevant to lysosome-related pathologies. Adapting the conceptual framework of intercellular gap junctions to organelle-organelle contacts offers a powerful, unexplored model to identify the molecular basis of this communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the vacuole inheritance proteins Vac8p, Vac9p, and Vac12p form a tethering complex at vacuole-vacuole contact sites that organizes a selective, regulated molecular channel, functionally analogous to a gap junction, to enable direct content transfer.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted genetic screen in S. cerevisiae using known vac mutants (e.g., vac8Δ, vac9Δ, vac12Δ) and fluorescent cargo reporters (e.g., FM4-64, GFP-tagged vacuolar hydrolases) to assess intervacuole content transfer via live-cell microscopy and fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP). Biochemical approaches, including co-immunoprecipitation and blue native PAGE, will test for stable complex formation between Vac8p, Vac9p, and Vac12p. High-resolution electron microscopy of contact sites in wild-type and mutant strains will visualize putative channel structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel molecular mechanism for direct communication between yeast vacuoles, fundamentally advancing the field of organelle biology. It will provide the first evidence for a 'vacuolar gap junction,' linking known inheritance machinery to a specific transport function and creating a new paradigm for studying inter-organelle signaling.
proposals.novelty: While Vac proteins are known for inheritance roles, their potential to form a regulated pore for content transfer is a novel, non-obvious function. Applying the well-defined biophysical principles of gap junctions to an intravacuolar problem represents a significant conceptual leap from established models of vesicular or tubular transport.

proposals.title: Network Flow Analysis of Vacuolar Transport: Modeling Cargo Diversion in a Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) Mutant Using Traffic Engineering Principles
proposals.motivation: The 'vac' mutant, defective in the direct Cvt pathway, exhibits a severe vacuole inheritance phenotype, yet the systems-level consequences of this single 'node' failure are poorly understood. Pre-2026 work has identified alternative pathways like autophagy and endosomal transport, but a quantitative, predictive model of how cargo flow is rerouted is lacking. This gap prevents a full understanding of cellular logistics and why the Cvt pathway is so critical for vacuole biogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 'vac' mutant phenotype results not just from the loss of the direct Cvt route, but from a systemic failure in the vacuolar logistics network where alternative pathways are insufficiently robust to handle the diverted cargo flow, leading to critical bottlenecks and cargo-specific delivery failures.
proposals.method: We will first map the vacuolar transport network by quantifying the flux of key cargoes (e.g., Ape1, Ams1) through all known pathways (Cvt, macroautophagy, microautophagy, endosome-Golgi) in wild-type and 'vac' mutant yeast using established biochemical and fluorescence microscopy assays. This quantitative flow data will parameterize an agent-based model (built using NetLogo) where cargo particles traverse a network of cellular compartments. We will implement network flow algorithms (e.g., Ford-Fulkerson for max flow/min cut analysis) to identify predicted bottlenecks and simulate the impact of node/edge failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, quantitative model of vacuolar cargo logistics, moving beyond pathway-centric views. It will predict which cargoes are most vulnerable to transport disruptions and identify the network properties that make vacuole inheritance robust or fragile. This will explain the 'vac' mutant phenotype at a systems level and establish a framework for analyzing other cellular logistics networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal traffic engineering and network flow theory to a cell biological problem, treating the cell as a logistics network to model phenotype emergence. This cross-disciplinary analogy provides a non-obvious, predictive framework distinct from traditional genetic or biochemical approaches to pathway analysis.

proposals.title: Defining the Shared Molecular Infrastructure and Regulatory Logic Coordinating Vacuolar Protein Sorting and Inheritance
proposals.motivation: The Vps pathway delivers hydrolases to the vacuole for homeostasis, while a separate inheritance machinery ensures organelle partitioning during division. How the cell avoids conflict and coordinates these two essential processes at the same membrane is unknown. This represents a critical gap in understanding organelle biogenesis and inheritance, analogous to managing a main supply chain alongside a timed delivery system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific Vps proteins, membrane lipids, and Rab GTPases function as shared infrastructure, with cell-cycle-dependent phosphorylation acting as a regulatory switch to re-route cargo and machinery from homeostatic sorting to inheritance-directed budding.
proposals.method: We will systematically screen vps and inheritance mutants for synthetic defects using fluorescence microscopy of cargo trafficking (e.g., GFP-Ape1) and vacuole segregation. Shared components will be identified via co-immunoprecipitation and yeast two-hybrid assays between core machinery (e.g., Vps proteins and Myo2). The regulatory logic will be tested by mapping phosphorylation sites on shared hubs (like a specific Vps protein or Vac8) in synchronized cells and analyzing the functional impact of phosphomimetic and non-phosphorylatable mutants on cargo routing priority.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrated molecular map of the vacuole biogenesis-inheritance interface, identifying key decision points. It will establish a mechanistic model for how organelle homeostasis and duplication are temporally coordinated, a fundamental principle of cell biology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a logistics network framework to dissect pathway intersection, moving beyond studying each pathway in isolation to explicitly test for shared components and switching mechanisms, a concept not previously applied to vacuole dynamics.

proposals.title: Reconstitution of Vacuole Motility Using a Minimal, Defined System of Purified Components
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance is a critical, actin- and microtubule-dependent process in yeast, but its molecular machinery remains incompletely defined. Current in vitro motility assays rely on crude cytosol, which contains redundant and confounding factors, obscuring the essential core components. A reductionist, synthetic biology-inspired approach is needed to definitively identify the minimal biochemical requirements for organelle motility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directed vacuole motility can be reconstituted in vitro using purified vacuoles and a minimal set of defined cytosolic proteins, including specific motor proteins, adaptors, and regulatory factors. This minimal system will be sufficient to support ATP-dependent movement along purified cytoskeletal tracks.
proposals.method: We will isolate yeast vacuoles from wild-type and motility-defective mutants (e.g., vac mutants). A curated set of candidate proteins—including the class V myosin Myo2, its vacuole-specific receptor Vac17, the tethering complex HOPS, plus kinesin and dynein motors with their adaptors—will be purified. Motility will be assayed in a flow chamber with immobilized, polarity-marked microtubules or actin cables, combining purified vacuoles, the defined protein set, an ATP-regenerating system, and imaging via fluorescence microscopy. Systematic omission/addition experiments will identify essential components.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first fully defined, minimal system for organelle motility, moving the field beyond crude extract assays. It will provide a definitive parts list for vacuole inheritance and create a powerful reductionist platform to dissect the roles of specific proteins and test mechanistic models of motor recruitment and regulation.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic approaches have been applied to minimal gene expression systems, applying this rigorous, bottom-up reconstitution philosophy to the problem of organelle motility is novel. It directly tests sufficiency in a way that bulk cytosol complementation assays cannot.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Intervacuole Communication in Saccharomyces cerevisiae Using a Dual-Compartment FRAP Assay
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance and communication are critical for cellular homeostasis, yet the kinetics of membrane and content exchange between vacuoles remain poorly quantified. Pre-2026 work has identified key proteins and pathways, like the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway, but lacks direct, dynamic measurements of intervacuole transport. A quantitative assay is needed to dissect the roles of known mutants (e.g., in homotypic fusion and vacuole protein sorting genes) and to characterize novel defective pathways affecting vacuolar dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fluorescence recovery after photobleaching (FRAP) applied to a dual-compartment vacuolar reporter can independently quantify the kinetics of membrane fusion and luminal content mixing between yeast vacuoles, providing a direct measure of intervacuole communication efficiency.
proposals.method: We will construct a yeast strain expressing a dual-fluorescence reporter: a vacuolar membrane protein (e.g., GFP-tagged Vph1p) and a soluble luminal protein (e.g., RFP-tagged carboxypeptidase Y). Using confocal microscopy, we will perform FRAP on a single vacuole within a connected network. The recovery kinetics of the membrane probe will be fitted with a diffusion model to derive a rate constant for membrane fusion/continuity. Simultaneously, the recovery of the luminal probe, which requires content mixing, will be analyzed to derive a rate constant for luminal exchange. These measurements will be repeated in wild-type and relevant mutant backgrounds (e.g., vps, vac, atg mutants).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, quantitative assay for intervacuole communication, enabling precise characterization of mutants affecting vacuolar dynamics. It will disentangle the contributions of membrane fusion events from content mixing, providing a foundational tool to dissect the molecular machinery of vacuole inheritance, autophagy, and organelle communication.
proposals.novelty: While FRAP is a classic technique for measuring intercellular transport, its adaptation to simultaneously probe two distinct organelle compartments (membrane and lumen) to independently quantify fusion and mixing kinetics is novel. This approach moves beyond static colocalization or biochemical fractionation to provide dynamic, kinetic parameters for vacuolar communication.

proposals.title: In Vitro Reconstitution of Vacuole Inheritance: A FRAP-Based Quantitative Analysis of Motility and Content Exchange
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in yeast is a critical, highly regulated process requiring coordinated organelle movement and membrane fusion. While in vivo studies have identified key proteins and pathways, including cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) and autophagy-related factors, the precise mechanistic coupling between vacuole motility and functional maturation remains unclear. A major barrier is the inability to isolate and manipulate individual components within the complex cellular environment to define their specific kinetic contributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal, reconstituted system of purified vacuoles, defined cytosolic factors (e.g., motor proteins, tethering complexes), and artificial microtubule tracks will recapitulate core inheritance motility. Furthermore, we posit that Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching (FRAP) applied within this system will reveal that the kinetics of membrane and luminal content exchange are directly modulated by specific motility and tethering factors.
proposals.method: We will isolate vacuoles from S. cerevisiae strains, including mutants defective in known inheritance or Cvt pathway components (e.g., vac mutants). These will be combined with purified cytosolic fractions or recombinant proteins (e.g., Myo2, Vac8, HOPS complex subunits) on surface-immobilized, polarity-marked microtubules. Motility events will be tracked via time-lapse microscopy of fluorescently labeled vacuoles. Subsequently, FRAP will be performed on a defined region of a moving vacuole or between two tethered vacuoles, using dual labeling of membrane (e.g., FM4-64) and luminal content (e.g., GFP-tagged hydrolases like Ape1). Custom algorithms will quantify recovery half-times and mobile fractions for each fluorophore under different biochemical perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, minimal in vitro system for vacuole inheritance, enabling direct measurement of the coupling between organelle transport and functional communication. It will define the specific kinetic roles of core machinery (e.g., motor proteins, tethers) in facilitating content exchange, moving beyond correlative in vivo observations. The findings will provide a foundational framework for understanding how organelle identity is maintained during active inheritance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of a reconstituted motility assay with quantitative FRAP kinetics to dissect inheritance, an approach not previously applied to yeast vacuoles. This allows for the first time the isolation of motility and fusion functions from the confounding regulatory networks of the intact cell.

proposals.title: Mapping the Context-Dependent Genetic Interaction Landscape of the VAC8-VAC12 Complex in Vacuole Inheritance and Membrane Contact Site Regulation
proposals.motivation: The VAC8-VAC12 complex is crucial for vacuole inheritance and membrane contact site formation, yet its functional genetic network is poorly defined. Pre-2026 work shows that genetic interactions are often condition-specific, and a single-condition map may miss critical, physiologically relevant interactions that define robustness or vulnerability in pathways like autophagy and organelle inheritance. A systematic, multi-context analysis is needed to understand how cellular environment and genetic background shape the functional relationships of this key complex.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VAC8-VAC12 complex participates in a core set of essential genetic interactions, but that a significant fraction of its genetic network is conditional, becoming apparent only under specific environmental stresses or in specific mutant backgrounds that mimic defective cellular states.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput Synthetic Genetic Array (SGA) analysis in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* to cross a query strain lacking VAC8 or VAC12 into the yeast knockout collection. Parallel screens will be conducted under standard conditions, nitrogen starvation (to induce autophagy), rapamycin treatment (TOR inhibition), and in genetic backgrounds with mutations in key vacuolar protein sorting (VPS) genes and contact site regulators (e.g., NVJ2). Genetic interaction scores will be calculated using established algorithms (e.g., ε-score) to identify synthetic sick/lethal and epistatic interactions, which will then be clustered to distinguish core from context-dependent interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive, context-aware genetic interaction map for the VAC8-VAC12 complex, directly linking it to specific stress response and trafficking pathways. It will identify novel genetic buffers and modifiers of vacuole-related phenotypes, providing a functional blueprint for how organelle inheritance and contact sites are integrated into broader cellular homeostasis.
proposals.novelty: While SGA is established, applying it in a multi-layered, parallel fashion across diverse genetic and environmental contexts to deconvolute core versus conditional interactions for a vacuolar complex is a novel and powerful approach. This moves beyond static interaction maps to reveal the dynamic genetic wiring that underlies cellular adaptation.

proposals.title: A Synthetic Genetic Array Analysis of VAC8-VAC12 Reveals Novel Genetic Interactions with Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting and Stress Response Pathways
proposals.motivation: The VAC8-VAC12 complex is crucial for vacuolar inheritance and membrane dynamics in yeast, but its broader functional network remains poorly defined. Pre-2026 work from the Klionsky lab established the existence of alternative protein sorting pathways to the vacuole, yet a systematic genetic interrogation of key vacuolar inheritance proteins is lacking. Mapping the genetic interactome of VAC8-VAC12 will illuminate its functional crosstalk with known trafficking and stress pathways, potentially revealing new regulatory nodes for vacuolar homeostasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VAC8-VAC12 complex exhibits specific, non-redundant genetic interactions with components of the cytoplasm-to-vacuole (Cvt) pathway and stress-responsive signaling networks, which are essential for maintaining vacuolar function under nutrient-limited conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput Synthetic Genetic Array (SGA) analysis in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, crossing query strains (Δvac8, Δvac12, and a double mutant) with the yeast non-essential gene deletion library. Genetic interactions will be quantified using established colony size analysis algorithms (e.g., SGAtools pipeline) to identify synthetic sick/lethal and suppressor interactions. Hits will be validated through manual tetrad dissection and phenotypic assays for Cvt pathway function (e.g., processing of Ape1) and vacuolar morphology under stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive genetic interaction map for the VAC8-VAC12 complex, directly linking vacuolar inheritance machinery to specific cargo delivery and stress adaptation pathways. It will identify novel genetic modifiers that could explain the 'defective' or 'pain' phenotypes observed in certain vacuolar mutants, providing a functional context for these observations.
proposals.novelty: While SGA is a standard method, it has not been systematically applied to dissect the genetic network of the VAC8-VAC12 complex in the context of alternative vacuolar targeting pathways. This approach moves beyond single-gene studies to reveal the system-level genetic buffering and vulnerabilities of a core vacuolar inheritance module.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Microfluidic Platform for 4D Quantification of Vacuole Inheritance Fidelity in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in yeast is a critical, highly regulated process, yet current studies rely on low-throughput, static imaging or manual pedigree analysis, limiting statistical power and temporal resolution. Understanding how environmental cues and genetic pathways, like the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway, coordinate organelle inheritance with cell cycle progression remains a fundamental challenge in cell biology. A systematic, quantitative approach is needed to dissect the fidelity and dynamics of this process under controlled conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vacuole inheritance fidelity is a tunable process, directly modulated by specific environmental stresses and dependent on functional Cvt and autophagy-related pathways for proper vacuolar protein sorting and membrane dynamics during division.
proposals.method: We will engineer a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device with hydrodynamic cell traps to immobilize and culture GFP-tagged vacuole membrane protein (Vph1-GFP) yeast strains for multi-generational imaging. An integrated, automated confocal microscope will acquire 3D z-stacks at 5-minute intervals over 12+ hours. Custom image analysis pipelines in FIJI/ImageJ, employing 3D segmentation (like the 3D Objects Counter plugin) and tracking algorithms, will quantify vacuole number, volume, and spatial distribution in mother and daughter cells through successive divisions. Defined media perturbations and isogenic mutants (e.g., apg/cvt mutants) will be perfused to test specific effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-throughput, quantitative atlas of normal and defective vacuole inheritance dynamics, directly linking inheritance fidelity to genetic and environmental factors. It will provide unprecedented statistical datasets to model organelle partitioning rules and identify new phenotypic classes for known autophagy/vacuolar mutants, bridging the gap between protein sorting pathways and organelle population dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of microfluidic cell pedigree analysis with high-temporal-resolution 3D confocal imaging specifically for the vacuole, enabling the automated, quantitative tracking of organelle populations across generations—a significant leap from snapshot or 2D analyses.

proposals.title: A Genome-Wide Screen for Vacuolar Import and Compartmentation (VAC) Mutants in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy, essential for cellular homeostasis and implicated in human diseases like neurodegeneration. While pioneering work identified core Cvt components, a comprehensive genetic landscape of vacuolar biogenesis and protein sorting remains incomplete. A systematic, genome-wide screen is needed to uncover novel VAC genes, potentially revealing new regulatory nodes and mechanistic links to other cellular pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genome-wide screen using a sensitive, quantitative assay for vacuolar hydrolase import will identify a broad set of novel VAC mutants, including genes involved in non-canonical targeting pathways, membrane dynamics, and regulatory signaling.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput, fluorescence-based assay in the yeast deletion mutant collection, monitoring the mislocalization of a chromosomally integrated GFP-tagged aminopeptidase I (Ape1) reporter, a canonical Cvt cargo. Automated microscopy and image analysis pipelines (using CellProfiler) will quantify vacuolar versus cytosolic GFP signal. Secondary validation will involve biochemical assays for prApe1 maturation and complementary microscopy with FM4-64 vacuolar membrane staining to classify mutants as specifically defective in targeting (e.g., vac mutants) versus general vacuolar biogenesis (vps mutants).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first complete catalog of VAC genes in yeast, significantly expanding the known molecular machinery for vacuolar import. It will provide a foundational resource for the autophagy and membrane trafficking fields, enabling detailed mechanistic studies of newly identified factors and their potential orthologs in higher eukaryotes.
proposals.novelty: Previous screens for autophagy/vacuolar mutants were often limited in scope or based on cumbersome assays. This proposal leverages a sensitive, automated, genome-wide approach specifically designed to isolate mutants defective in the import of a soluble hydrolase, moving beyond known autophagy (ATG) genes to systematically map the entire VAC network.

proposals.title: Environmental Modulation of V-ATPase Function Revealed by Microfluidic Analysis of Vacuole Inheritance in Engineered Yeast
proposals.motivation: The V-ATPase is a conserved rotary machine essential for vacuolar acidification and organelle inheritance. While its c-ring stoichiometry is genetically fixed, determining the proton:ATP ratio, it is unknown if environmental factors can modulate its *effective* functional output in vivo. This gap limits our understanding of how cells dynamically regulate organelle biogenesis and inheritance in response to stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that environmental parameters (e.g., external pH, ionic strength) can modulate the effective proton translocation efficiency of the V-ATPase in vivo, despite its fixed structural stoichiometry, and that this modulation will be directly quantifiable as a defect in vacuole inheritance fidelity under controlled conditions.
proposals.method: We will engineer *S. cerevisiae* strains where vacuole inheritance is sensitized via mutations in alternative vacuolar protein sorting pathways (e.g., *vps* mutants) and coupled to V-ATPase function using known vacuole inheritance mutants (*vac* mutants). Isogenic strains with wild-type and altered c-ring subunit counts (e.g., *vma3* mutants) will be immobilized in microfluidic traps. Using custom Python-based image analysis pipelines (leveraging CellProfiler and TrackMate), we will subject cells to dynamically controlled environmental perturbations while performing long-term, single-cell timelapse microscopy to quantitatively score vacuole inheritance failure as a proxy for V-ATPase functional output.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between the mechanochemical performance of a rotary molecular machine and an observable organelle dynamics phenotype. It will reveal whether environmental cues can induce functional plasticity in an otherwise structurally rigid machine, providing a new paradigm for understanding cellular adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While V-ATPase structure and vacuole inheritance are well-studied separately, this project uniquely fuses them by using organelle dynamics as a sensitive, real-time biosensor for rotary machine function under environmental modulation, an approach not previously applied in this context.

proposals.title: Conserved Core Machinery for Vacuole Fusion: Testing the Requirement of HOPS and SNAREs in Post-Inheritance Intervacuole Communication
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance in yeast is a critical, yet mechanistically unresolved, process ensuring organelle continuity after cell division. While homotypic vacuole fusion is well-characterized, the molecular basis for the initial communication and content exchange between the inherited maternal vacuole and the newly formed vacuole is unknown. Determining if this process repurposes the known homotypic fusion machinery would provide a fundamental principle for organelle inheritance, analogous to the shared core components found in diverse neuronal vesicle fusion events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core membrane fusion machinery, specifically the HOPS tethering complex and the vacuolar SNARE complex (e.g., Vam3, Vam7, Vti1, Nyv1), essential for homotypic vacuole fusion, is also required for the initial docking and fusion events between maternal and nascent vacuoles following inheritance.
proposals.method: We will employ comparative in vitro fusion assays using purified vacuoles from wild-type and specific mutant strains (e.g., vam3Δ, vps11Δ). Vacuoles will be isolated from mother and daughter cell populations after cytokinesis using established fractionation techniques. Fusion efficiency will be quantified via a content-mixing assay based on the maturation of pro-alkaline phosphatase. Genetic and biochemical approaches will dissect requirements: vacuoles from mutants defective in homotypic fusion will be tested in the inheritance-specific assay, and dominant-negative SNARE fragments will be used to inhibit specific complex formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a unifying model for vacuole membrane dynamics, determining if a single core fusion apparatus serves multiple cellular functions. It will identify whether unique regulatory factors (e.g., specific Rab GTPases or phosphoinositides) are required to specify the inheritance context. The findings will significantly advance our understanding of organelle biogenesis and stability.
proposals.novelty: While vacuole inheritance mutants exist, no study has directly tested if the well-defined homotypic fusion machinery is functionally deployed in this distinct cellular context. The explicit analogy to synaptic vesicle machinery, where core components are shared but regulated differently, provides a novel conceptual framework for investigating organelle inheritance.

proposals.title: Acute, Reversible Vacuole Mis-Segregation via Optogenetic Tethering Reveals Immediate Post-Mitotic Consequences for Cellular Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: The vacuole is a critical organelle for pH regulation, osmoregulation, and autophagy in yeast, yet the specific consequences of its asymmetric inheritance during mitosis remain poorly understood. Classic genetic mutants with constitutive vacuole inheritance defects cause pleiotropic and compensatory effects, obscuring the direct, acute roles of vacuolar inheritance in daughter cell physiology. An acute, reversible perturbation system is needed to isolate these immediate effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute, reversible mis-segregation of vacuoles during mitosis will cause immediate and specific defects in cytoplasmic pH buffering, osmotic balance, and autophagic capacity in the newborn daughter cell, independent of general stress responses.
proposals.method: We will engineer Saccharomyces cerevisiae strains expressing a vacuole membrane-targeted component (CIB1 fused to a vacuolar targeting signal from aminopeptidase I or Vph1) and a cortex/anchor-targeted component (Cry2 fused to a mother-cell cortical protein, such as Bud6). Using time-lapse microscopy, we will illuminate dividing cells with 488 nm light to induce heterodimerization and tether vacuoles to the mother cortex, then cease illumination to reverse tethering. We will quantify mis-segregation efficiency and simultaneously monitor real-time reporters for vacuolar pH (e.g., pHluorin), osmotic stress (Hog1-GFP localization), and autophagic flux (GFP-Atg8 processing).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel optogenetic tool for acute organelle mis-segregation, moving beyond constitutive mutants. It will directly define the immediate post-mitotic requirements for vacuolar inheritance, linking specific vacuole-depletion states to defects in homeostasis pathways. This provides a causal framework for understanding how organelle inheritance ensures daughter cell fitness.
proposals.novelty: While vacuole inheritance mutants exist, this approach achieves acute, reversible, and spatially precise mis-segregation only during mitosis, allowing high-temporal-resolution analysis of primary defects without long-term compensatory adaptations seen in constitutive mutants.

proposals.title: Inducible Vacuole Clustering via Synthetic Adhesives Reveals Scaling Consequences for Cellular Homeostasis
proposals.motivation: Vacuole inheritance is a critical, tightly regulated process in yeast, but the systemic consequences of its failure are not fully understood. Pre-2026 work from the Klionsky lab and others has identified key pathways like the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway and characterized mutants with defective vacuolar segregation, yet tools to acutely and reversibly induce this defect are lacking. This limits our ability to dissect the downstream cascades of stress, metabolic disruption, and inter-organelle communication triggered by vacuolar 'traffic jams.'
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acute, inducible cross-linking of vacuoles will create a controlled inheritance blockade, and that the cellular response to this engineered defect will reveal novel scaling relationships and compensatory pathways distinct from those observed in chronic genetic mutants.
proposals.method: We will engineer a synthetic molecular adhesive by fusing the vacuolar membrane protein Vph1 to chemically (e.g., FKBP-FRB) or optogenetically (e.g., Cry2-CIB1) dimerizable domains. In budding yeast, induction will cause homotypic vacuole-vacuole adhesion, mimicking a segregation defect. We will use live-cell microscopy to quantify the dynamics of this 'traffic jam' and its resolution. Concurrently, we will employ transcriptomic and metabolomic profiling, comparing our inducible system to classical vacuole inheritance mutants (e.g., vac mutants) to identify acute versus chronic response pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, tunable tool for perturbing organelle dynamics with temporal precision, moving beyond static mutants. It will define the immediate transcriptional and metabolic consequences of vacuole mis-segregation, potentially linking vacuolar 'traffic' to broader cellular homeostasis networks. The findings will provide a new inverse problem framework for understanding how cells sense and scale their response to organelle-level disruptions.
proposals.novelty: While vacuole inheritance mutants are well-studied, the use of inducible synthetic adhesives to create a reversible, acute 'traffic jam' is a novel perturbation strategy. This approach allows us to separate the primary effects of the segregation defect from secondary adaptations seen in chronic mutants, a distinction not previously possible.

proposals.title: Direct Cytosol-to-Vacuole Transport of Aminopeptidase I: A Kinetic and Ultrastructural Analysis to Exclude an Intermediate Compartment
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy and unconventional protein trafficking in yeast. While the Cvt pathway is described as a direct route from the cytosol to the vacuole, the precise mechanism of vesicle formation and delivery remains incompletely resolved. Determining whether the cargo, aminopeptidase I (API), transits through an intermediate compartment like the Golgi or endosome is critical for understanding the fundamental topology and regulation of this pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that API is delivered directly from the cytosol to the vacuole without passing through a canonical membrane-bound intermediate compartment. If an intermediate compartment were involved, we would detect kinetic delays and specific vesicular intermediates distinct from autophagosomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of pulse-chase radiolabeling with immunoprecipitation and quantitative fluorescence microscopy to establish the precise kinetics of API maturation. To probe for intermediates, we will utilize yeast mutants defective in Golgi (sec7), endosomal (vps4), and autophagic (atg1) pathways, assessing API processing and localization via immuno-EM and colocalization studies with compartment-specific markers (e.g., Pep12, Vps10). A key approach will be the use of a temperature-sensitive sec18 (NSF) mutant to synchronize traffic and trap potential ER/Golgi intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the 'direct delivery' model of the Cvt pathway, providing crucial kinetic and morphological evidence. It will clarify the relationship between the Cvt pathway and other trafficking routes, strengthening its utility as a model for selective autophagy. The findings will also inform our understanding of how cytosolic cargo is recognized and sequestered.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identified the Cvt pathway and its determinants, a systematic, multi-method investigation to exclude all known intermediate compartments has not been conducted. This proposal directly challenges the possibility of a cryptic intermediate step in a pathway defined by its directness.

proposals.title: Functional Specificity in Vacuolar Zymogen Activation: Assessing the Protease Redundancy for Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting Substrate Aminopeptidase I
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy, delivering precursor aminopeptidase I (prAPI) to the vacuole for maturation. While Proteinase B (Prb1) is known to process prAPI, the functional redundancy of the vacuolar protease network remains unclear. Determining if other proteases can compensate for Prb1 deficiency is critical for understanding the robustness of vacuolar protein processing and the specificity of autophagy-related proteolytic events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Proteinase B is uniquely required for the cleavage of the propeptide of API upon its delivery to the vacuole via the Cvt pathway, and that other major vacuolar proteases (Proteinase A, Carboxypeptidase Y, Carboxypeptidase S) cannot process this substrate in its absence.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of Saccharomyces cerevisiae protease-deficient mutants (e.g., prb1Δ, pep4Δ, prb1Δpep4Δ, cps1Δ) in combination with API processing assays. Processing of prAPI will be monitored by immunoblotting with anti-API antibodies following vacuolar isolation or cell fractionation. We will also employ pulse-chase analysis with 35S-methionine to track the kinetics of prAPI maturation in single and double protease mutants. The localization and stability of prAPI will be further assessed in these mutant backgrounds using fluorescence microscopy of API-GFP constructs.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the protease specificity for a major Cvt pathway substrate, clarifying a key step in vacuolar biogenesis. It will reveal the degree of redundancy within the vacuolar hydrolytic system for processing autophagic cargo, informing models of organelle assembly and quality control. The findings may also explain previously observed phenotypic differences between prb1Δ and other protease mutants.
proposals.novelty: While Prb1's role in API processing is documented, a systematic genetic test for functional redundancy among all major vacuolar proteases in this specific context has not been performed. This directly tests the long-standing assumption of Prb1's unique specificity versus potential backup mechanisms within the protease network.

proposals.title: Identification of a Cytosolic Receptor for the Essential N-terminal Alpha-Helix of Aminopeptidase I in the Cvt Pathway
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy in yeast, delivering the hydrolase aminopeptidase I (API) to the vacuole. While the first alpha-helix of the API propeptide is known to be essential for targeting, the specific receptor that recognizes this determinant remains unidentified, representing a critical gap in understanding the molecular initiation of this pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific cytosolic or vacuolar membrane protein acts as a receptor that directly binds the essential first alpha-helix of the API propeptide, facilitating its recognition and subsequent packaging into Cvt vesicles.
proposals.method: We will employ a comprehensive two-pronged approach. First, a yeast two-hybrid screen will be performed using the essential 20-amino acid alpha-helical region of the API propeptide as bait against a yeast genomic library. Second, we will conduct a targeted biochemical screen using the biotinylated propeptide helix to affinity-purify interacting proteins from yeast cytosol, followed by identification via mass spectrometry. Candidate receptors will be validated through genetic interaction studies with known cvt mutants and in vitro binding assays using purified components.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the primary receptor for the major Cvt cargo, API, thereby defining the initial recognition step in this selective autophagy-related pathway. It will provide a crucial missing link between cargo recognition and the known autophagy-related (Atg) protein machinery, advancing our mechanistic understanding of selective autophagic targeting.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identified the targeting determinant in API and many downstream Atg proteins, the direct receptor that 'sees' this signal has remained elusive, making its discovery a novel and logical next step in the field.

proposals.title: The Role of Precursor Aminopeptidase I Folding State and Propeptide Determinants in Cvt Pathway Recognition and Import
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy in yeast, responsible for delivering hydrolases like aminopeptidase I (API) to the vacuole. While the propeptide of API is known to be essential for its targeting, the precise molecular mechanism by which the Cvt complex recognizes the API precursor (prAPI) remains unclear. Understanding how the folding state of prAPI and specific residues within its propeptide dictate recognition is critical for elucidating the specificity of this fundamental transport pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the folding state of prAPI, modulated by specific propeptide mutations, directly influences its recognition by the Cvt complex, where a partially folded, import-competent conformation is required for efficient binding and subsequent vacuolar import.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of genetic, biochemical, and cell biological approaches. Site-directed mutagenesis will be used to generate a panel of propeptide mutants in the prAPI gene. Their import efficiency will be quantified in wild-type and autophagy-defective (atg) mutant strains using pulse-chase analysis and immunoblotting. The folding state of wild-type and mutant prAPI will be assessed using limited proteolysis and native gel electrophoresis. Co-immunoprecipitation assays will determine the binding affinity of these variants for core Cvt components, such as Atg19 and Atg11.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the structural features of prAPI required for Cvt pathway recognition, establishing a direct link between precursor folding and selective autophagy receptor binding. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how cytosolic cargo is selected for vacuolar delivery, with implications for understanding related pathways in higher eukaryotes.
proposals.novelty: While the propeptide's necessity is established, this study directly tests how its sequence dictates a specific folding intermediate required for receptor interaction, moving beyond mere mapping of targeting determinants to defining a conformational code for import.

proposals.title: Defining the Minimal Functional Sequence of the First Alpha-Helix in the Ape1 Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting Determinant
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy in yeast, with aminopeptidase I (Ape1) serving as a major cargo. While the N-terminal propeptide of Ape1 is known to contain a targeting determinant essential for Cvt vesicle formation, the precise molecular code within its first alpha-helix required for receptor binding and vacuolar delivery remains undefined. Mapping this minimal functional sequence is critical for understanding the specificity of cargo recognition, a fundamental but poorly resolved step in selective autophagy pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a short, contiguous cluster of hydrophobic and charged residues within the first alpha-helix of the Ape1 propeptide constitutes the essential core for binding to the autophagy receptor Atg19 and subsequent targeting via the Cvt pathway.
proposals.method: We will employ systematic alanine-scanning mutagenesis of the first alpha-helix (residues based on pre-2026 structural data) in the Ape1 propeptide fused to a reporter protein. Mutants will be expressed in relevant yeast autophagy mutants (e.g., Δape1) and assessed for vacuolar delivery using pulse-chase and protease protection assays. Defective targeting mutants will be analyzed for their interaction with Atg19 via co-immunoprecipitation. Computational modeling (using pre-2026 tools like PyMOL and helical wheel projections) will guide the design of targeted point mutations and compensatory charge-reversal experiments to validate specific residue interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will pinpoint the exact amino acid residues constituting the functional core of a canonical Cvt pathway targeting signal. It will provide a high-resolution map of a cargo-receptor interface in selective autophagy, establishing a paradigm for dissecting similar determinants in other pathways. The findings will directly inform models of how cargo sequence motifs are decoded by the autophagy machinery.
proposals.novelty: While the general region of the Ape1 propeptide is known to be important, defining the minimal functional sequence at single-residue resolution within its first alpha-helix is novel. This moves beyond identifying necessary regions to deciphering the specific physico-chemical grammar of the targeting signal, which has not been achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Identification of a Putative Receptor for Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) by Yeast Two-Hybrid Screening with the Aminopeptidase I Propeptide as Bait
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy in yeast, essential for understanding related pathways in higher eukaryotes, including mammalian macroautophagy. While the targeting determinant for the cargo aminopeptidase I (Ape1) is known to reside in its propeptide, the specific receptor protein(s) that recognize this signal and mediate vesicle docking or autophagosome formation remain unidentified. Identifying this receptor is a critical missing link in fully mapping the Cvt pathway's molecular machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: The propeptide of Ape1, which contains its essential targeting information, directly interacts with a specific receptor protein that facilitates its recognition and sequestration into Cvt vesicles. This putative receptor can be isolated using the propeptide as bait in a yeast two-hybrid screen.
proposals.method: We will employ the classic yeast two-hybrid system. The bait plasmid will express a fusion of the GAL4 DNA-binding domain with the full-length Ape1 propeptide (as defined in Klionsky et al., J. Cell Biol. 1992). This bait will be used to screen a high-complexity yeast genomic DNA activation domain fusion library. Positive clones will be selected based on histidine prototrophy and confirmed via β-galactosidase assays. Interacting proteins will be sequenced and validated through co-immunoprecipitation from yeast lysates and analysis of genetic interactions with known cvt and autophagy (atg) mutants.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a candidate receptor for the Cvt pathway, providing a direct molecular link between cargo recognition and the vesicle formation machinery. It will significantly advance the mechanistic understanding of selective autophagy and may reveal conserved principles applicable to mammalian systems. The findings could also explain the targeting defects observed in specific cvt mutants.
proposals.novelty: While the Ape1 propeptide's role as a targeting signal is established, a direct interaction screen for its cognate receptor has not been reported. This approach moves beyond genetic epistasis to identify a physical binding partner, offering a new and specific molecular target for functional dissection of the Cvt pathway's initial steps.

proposals.title: The Aminopeptidase I Propeptide is a Sufficient and Modular Signal for Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting of Heterologous Cytosolic Proteins
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a selective autophagy process in yeast, essential for vacuolar hydrolase delivery.
