While the propeptide of aminopeptidase I (API) is necessary for its own targeting via the Cvt pathway, its sufficiency as an independent, portable targeting tag for other proteins remains untested. Determining if the API propeptide can function as a generalizable vacuolar tag would advance our understanding of cargo recognition in selective autophagy and could provide a new tool for protein targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: The propeptide region of API contains all necessary information for Cvt pathway recognition and is sufficient to direct heterologous, normally cytosolic proteins to the vacuole.
proposals.method: We will construct N-terminal fusions of the API propeptide (residues 1-45) to well-characterized cytosolic reporter proteins (e.g., GFP, Pho8Δ60, or a cytosolic mutant of a non-Cvt cargo). These constructs will be expressed in wild-type and key Cvt pathway mutant strains (e.g., Δatg19, Δatg11). Vacuolar delivery will be assessed via fluorescence microscopy (for GFP fusions), protease protection assays on isolated vacuoles, and immunoblot analysis to monitor processing from precursor to mature form, a hallmark of successful Cvt delivery.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether the API propeptide is a modular targeting determinant, a fundamental question in Cvt pathway mechanism. It will provide a novel genetic tool for redirecting cytosolic proteins to the vacuole for degradation or function. Furthermore, it will clarify the principles of cargo recognition by the Atg19 receptor and the Cvt complex.
proposals.novelty: Previous work identified the API propeptide as necessary for its own targeting, but its sufficiency as a portable tag for unrelated proteins is a direct and unresolved experimental question. Testing this in defined Cvt pathway mutants will distinguish true propeptide-mediated targeting from non-specific autophagy.

proposals.title: Conservation and Divergence of the Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) Pathway in Non-Saccharomyces Fungi
proposals.motivation: The Cvt pathway, first characterized in Saccharomyces cerevisiae for targeting aminopeptidase I (API), is a key model for selective autophagy and protein sorting. Its conservation beyond budding yeast is poorly understood, limiting our knowledge of its evolutionary role and functional diversity in eukaryotic cell biology. Investigating its presence in other fungi will reveal if it is a broadly conserved or a lineage-specific mechanism.
proposals.hypothesis: The core targeting determinant of API and the Cvt pathway machinery are functionally conserved in other fungal species, particularly within the Ascomycota phylum. Alternatively, the pathway may be absent or significantly divergent in species lacking a clear API homolog.
proposals.method: We will employ comparative genomics to identify putative API homologs in selected fungal genomes (e.g., Schizosaccharomyces pombe, Candida albicans, Aspergillus nidulans). For candidate proteins, we will construct GFP-fusion reporters and express them in both native and S. cerevisiae systems to assess vacuolar targeting. Genetic screens in the native hosts, using CRISPR-Cas9 (pre-2026 methodology) to generate mutants in putative Cvt orthologs (e.g., Atg11, Atg19), will test for defective targeting. Subcellular fractionation and protease protection assays will confirm delivery to the vacuole lumen.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the phylogenetic distribution of the Cvt pathway, establishing its evolutionary significance. It will identify conserved and novel components of the targeting machinery, potentially revealing new regulatory principles. The findings will expand the model systems available for studying selective autophagy and its connections to cellular homeostasis.
proposals.novelty: While API targeting is well-studied in S. cerevisiae, systematic investigation of its conservation across fungi has not been performed. This study moves beyond a single model organism to test the universality of a fundamental pathway, potentially discovering variant or simplified forms of the Cvt mechanism.

proposals.title: Mechanistic dissection of Vam3p delivery to the vacuole: defining the ALP pathway's role in CPY pathway function
proposals.motivation: In yeast, the CPY and ALP pathways are distinct vacuolar protein sorting routes. Vam3p, a t-SNARE essential for CPY pathway vesicle fusion, is itself delivered via the ALP pathway. This creates a critical interdependency, but the molecular mechanisms enabling this cross-pathway delivery remain undefined. Understanding this crosstalk is fundamental to mapping the integrated network of vacuolar biogenesis and membrane traffic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, conserved sorting determinants within the Vam3p cytoplasmic domain are recognized by the ALP machinery, and that loss of ALP function disrupts CPY delivery not merely by vacuole fragmentation, but by directly impairing Vam3p targeting and t-SNARE complex assembly.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted mutagenesis screen of the VAM3 gene to identify residues critical for its ALP-mediated delivery, using GFP-tagged constructs and fluorescence microscopy in wild-type and ALP pathway mutants (e.g., Δapl5). Biochemical assays will assess Vam3p stability and incorporation into SNARE complexes. Genetic epistasis analysis will test for synthetic defects between ALP mutants and CPY pathway (vps) mutants. A candidate-based approach will test for physical interactions between Vam3p and ALP-specific adaptors like Apl5p using co-immunoprecipitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular 'zip code' that targets a key t-SNARE via a non-canonical pathway, revealing a direct mechanistic link between two major vacuolar sorting routes. It will clarify whether ALP's role in CPY transport is solely structural (vacuole morphology) or also involves specific cargo recognition. The findings will provide a model for how organelle biogenesis pathways are functionally coupled.
proposals.novelty: While the ALP pathway's role in delivering Vam3p is known, the specific targeting determinants and adaptor interactions have not been mapped. This proposal moves beyond correlation to directly test the mechanism of this essential cross-pathway dependency, which is a non-obvious feature of the vacuolar sorting network.

proposals.title: Identification of a Conserved Cytoplasm-to-Lysosome Targeting (CLT) Pathway in Mammalian Cells
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway in yeast is a well-characterized, selective autophagy-like pathway for the constitutive delivery of hydrolases like aminopeptidase I (Ape1). It is widely assumed to be a yeast-specific innovation. However, the fundamental need for constitutive lysosomal delivery of specific hydrolases exists in all eukaryotes, and the evolutionary conservation of core autophagy machinery suggests related targeting mechanisms may exist in higher organisms, a possibility largely unexplored pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mammalian cells possess a constitutive, selective autophagy pathway functionally analogous to the yeast Cvt pathway, which delivers specific hydrolases to the lysosome independently of bulk autophagy induction.
proposals.method: We will employ a bioinformatic screen to identify mammalian proteins with structural motifs resembling the Ape1 propeptide, the key Cvt targeting determinant. Candidate proteins (e.g., procathepsins or other hydrolases) will be epitope-tagged and their localization tracked via immunofluorescence and subcellular fractionation in wild-type and autophagy-deficient (ATG5/ATG7 knockout) mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs). We will use site-directed mutagenesis of putative targeting motifs and perform co-immunoprecipitation assays to test for interaction with mammalian orthologs of yeast Cvt receptors like Atg19.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the paradigm of Cvt pathway exclusivity to yeast and identify the first components of a putative mammalian constitutive lysosomal targeting (CLT) pathway. It will expand our understanding of lysosomal biogenesis and provide a new framework for studying defective protein sorting in lysosomal storage disorders and other diseases.
proposals.novelty: While mammalian selective autophagy for degradation (e.g., mitophagy) is known, the search for a constitutive, biosynthetic counterpart to the yeast Cvt pathway is a novel and non-obvious direction, moving beyond degradation-focused models of autophagy-related pathways.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Assembly and Regulation of the Core Atg Machinery for Selective Cargo Delivery via the Cvt Pathway and Autophagy
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway and autophagy share a core set of Atg proteins, yet they deliver distinct cargoes under different nutrient conditions. Pre-2026 work has identified key components and targeting determinants, but the precise molecular mechanisms governing how this shared machinery assembles into functionally distinct complexes for biosynthetic Cvt versus degradative autophagy remain poorly defined. Understanding this regulatory switch is fundamental to cell biology and has implications for understanding autophagy-related diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the assembly, stoichiometry, and post-translational modifications of the core Atg machinery differ between the Cvt and autophagy pathways, directed by specific targeting determinants and nutrient-sensing kinases, to determine cargo specificity and pathway flux.
proposals.method: We will employ systematic, quantitative fluorescence microscopy (e.g., FRET, FLIM) and biochemical co-purification in S. cerevisiae to compare the spatiotemporal assembly of core Atg complexes (e.g., Atg1 complex, Atg8-PE conjugation system) during Cvt and nitrogen starvation-induced autophagy. This will be combined with targeted mutagenesis of known Cvt cargo determinants (like the Ape1 propeptide) and key regulatory kinases (Atg1, Torc1) to assess their role in complex assembly. Proteomic analysis of affinity-purified complexes under both conditions will identify condition-specific protein interactions and modifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed mechanistic map of how a conserved molecular toolkit is dynamically reconfigured for distinct cellular functions. It will define the regulatory checkpoints that commit shared components to either biosynthetic or degradative vacuolar delivery, resolving a long-standing question in the field. The findings will establish a new framework for understanding how pathway specificity emerges from a common proteome.
proposals.novelty: While the shared components are known, a direct, comparative analysis of their assembly dynamics and regulatory states in the Cvt versus autophagy pathways has not been performed. This proposal moves beyond cataloging components to defining the active regulatory principles of the machinery.

proposals.title: Harnessing the Constitutive Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) Pathway for the Clearance of Pathological Protein Aggregates in Yeast Models of Neurodegeneration
proposals.motivation: Pathological protein aggregation is a hallmark of neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's, and cellular clearance mechanisms are often overwhelmed. Pre-2026 work has established the yeast Cvt pathway as a constitutive, selective autophagy-like process for delivering specific hydrolases like aminopeptidase I (Ape1) to the vacuole. This pathway's inherent, non-inducible activity represents a potential untapped route for clearing aggregation-prone proteins before they form toxic oligomers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing aggregation-prone protein domains (e.g., huntingtin polyQ, α-synuclein) to the Ape1 propeptide, the known Cvt targeting determinant, will co-opt the constitutive Cvt machinery to deliver these pathogenic proteins to the vacuole for degradation, thereby reducing their cytosolic aggregation and associated toxicity.
proposals.method: We will generate yeast expression constructs fusing the Ape1 propeptide to fluorescently tagged aggregation-prone proteins (e.g., polyQ repeats, α-synuclein). Using established assays, we will monitor aggregate formation via fluorescence microscopy and filter-trap assays in wild-type and key Cvt pathway mutants (e.g., Δatg11, Δatg19). Vacuolar delivery and degradation will be assessed by colocalization with vacuolar markers, protease sensitivity assays, and monitoring of fluorescence signal upon vacuolar lysis. Toxicity will be measured by growth assays and sensitivity to stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for engineering a constitutive cellular clearance pathway to target aggregation-prone proteins. It will define the capacity and specificity limits of the Cvt pathway as a potential therapeutic scaffold. The findings could inform novel strategies for neuroprotection by enhancing pre-emptive clearance, distinct from inducing bulk autophagy.
proposals.novelty: While autophagy induction is a known strategy, directly hijacking a constitutive, selective delivery pathway like Cvt for pathogenic cargo clearance is novel. This approach bypasses the need for stress signaling and could provide continuous, baseline protection against proteotoxicity.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Non-Vacuolar Compartments for Basic Amino Acid and Polyphosphate Storage in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: The yeast vacuole is classically considered the primary storage site for basic amino acids like arginine and for inorganic polyphosphate (polyP). However, pre-2026 work from the Klionsky lab and others on alternative protein sorting pathways (e.g., cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting, Cvt) and vacuole biogenesis mutants suggests significant cellular plasticity. This raises a fundamental question: in the absence of a fully functional vacuole, or under specific metabolic conditions, do other organelles compensate for storage? Resolving this is critical for a complete understanding of nutrient homeostasis and organelle function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under conditions of vacuolar compromise or specific metabolic stress, alternative organelles, including the nucleus, mitochondria, and/or cytoplasmic granules, will serve as significant reservoirs for basic amino acids and polyphosphate.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of genetic, cell biological, and biochemical approaches in S. cerevisiae. First, we will use established vacuolar biogenesis and function mutants (e.g., vps, vam, and vtc strains) to disrupt canonical storage. Using fluorescent probes (e.g., polyphosphate-binding domain fusions) and compartment-specific markers, we will perform quantitative co-localization microscopy to identify alternative storage sites. We will complement this with subcellular fractionation and direct biochemical quantification of arginine and polyP in isolated organellar fractions from wild-type and mutant strains under varying nutrient conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly challenge and refine the canonical view of the vacuole as the sole major storage organelle for these metabolites. It will map a potential compensatory network for nutrient storage, linking vacuolar function to broader cellular economy. The findings could explain the viability of certain vacuolar mutants and reveal novel regulatory pathways for metabolic adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While the vacuole's role is well-established, a systematic search for alternative storage sites driven by vacuolar dysfunction is novel. This proposal directly extends the lab's expertise in vacuolar protein targeting and mutant analysis into the underexplored area of metabolite compartmentalization plasticity.

proposals.title: Genetic Dissection of Vacuolar Function: Isolation of Mutants with Discrete Defects in Storage, pH Regulation, and Proteolysis
proposals.motivation: The yeast vacuole is a multifunctional organelle critical for storage, ion homeostasis, pH regulation, and macromolecular degradation via autophagy and the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway. Pre-2026 models often treat these functions as an integrated network, but it remains unclear if they are mechanistically interdependent or can be genetically separated. Testing this is fundamental to understanding organelle evolution and the molecular basis of vacuole-related pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that core vacuolar functions—such as ion storage, pH homeostasis, and autophagic/Cvt proteolysis—are governed by discrete, genetically separable pathways, such that mutants can be isolated that are defective in one function while others remain intact.
proposals.method: We will employ a comprehensive genetic screen in *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* using a mutagenized library of strains harboring reporters for discrete functions: a pH-sensitive GFP for luminal acidity, polyphosphate staining for storage, and processing of Ape1 (via the Cvt pathway) and GFP-Atg8 (via autophagy) for proteolysis. High-throughput fluorescence microscopy and automated image analysis will quantify each parameter. Mutants will be isolated by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and subjected to complementation analysis and whole-genome sequencing to identify causative lesions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive genetic map of functional independence within the vacuole, identifying novel genes and alleles that specifically uncouple storage, pH control, and degradation. It will clarify whether vacuolar dysfunction in disease models stems from global collapse or specific pathway failure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized mutants broadly defective in vacuolar biogenesis (e.g., *vps* mutants), a systematic screen for mutants that cleanly separate these core physiological functions, rather than general trafficking, has not been conducted, challenging the assumption of their obligatory interconnection.

proposals.title: Identification of Vacuolar Targeting and Regulatory Pathways for Polyphosphate Storage and Mobilization in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: Inorganic polyphosphate (polyP) is a crucial energy and phosphate reservoir stored in the vacuole, yet the molecular mechanisms governing its dynamic storage and release remain poorly defined. Pre-2026 work has established the vacuole's central role in nutrient homeostasis and identified specific protein sorting pathways like the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway, but analogous pathways for polyP regulation are unknown. Understanding these mechanisms is fundamental to cellular adaptation to nutrient stress and has implications for processes where polyP is implicated, such as phosphate signaling and microbial stress tolerance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific vacuolar membrane proteins and targeting pathways, potentially analogous to the Cvt pathway, regulate the dynamic import and export of polyphosphate in response to cellular phosphate status. Furthermore, we predict that mutants defective in known vacuolar protein sorting will exhibit aberrant polyP storage dynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of genetic, biochemical, and imaging approaches in S. cerevisiae. First, we will screen a panel of existing vacuolar protein sorting (vps) and autophagy (atg) mutants, defective in pathways like the Cvt, for altered polyP content using DAPI-based fluorescence assays and biochemical extraction. Second, we will use a candidate approach to test if polyP metabolism enzymes (e.g., Vtc complex, Ppn1) contain specific vacuolar targeting determinants, employing GFP-fusion constructs and mutagenesis of putative targeting sequences, similar to the methodology used to identify the aminopeptidase I Cvt determinant. Third, live-cell microscopy with polyP-binding domain probes will visualize real-time polyP granule dynamics in these mutants under phosphate-rich and phosphate-starved conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly link the well-characterized field of vacuolar protein sorting to the understudied area of inorganic polymer metabolism. It will identify specific genetic factors and potential targeting signals required for polyP storage and mobilization, establishing a new regulatory paradigm for vacuolar phosphate homeostasis. The findings will provide a mechanistic framework for how cells dynamically manage this critical energy and phosphate reserve.
proposals.novelty: While vacuolar protein import for hydrolases is well-studied, applying this conceptual framework to the regulation of a small-molecule polymer like polyphosphate is a novel and non-obvious extension. The proposal uniquely bridges Dr. Klionsky's expertise in vacuolar targeting pathways with the biochemistry of polyP, a connection not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive Proteomic Analysis of the Yeast Vacuole: Defining the Steady-State Cargo and Identifying Novel Targeting Pathways
proposals.motivation: The yeast vacuole is a critical organelle for autophagy, protein turnover, and nutrient homeostasis. While pathways like the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) and autophagy have been characterized using specific cargoes like aminopeptidase I, a complete, unbiased inventory of vacuolar resident proteins is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of the vacuole's full functional repertoire and the diversity of protein targeting mechanisms that sustain it.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic proteomic analysis of highly purified vacuoles will reveal a substantial cohort of previously uncharacterized resident proteins, including novel substrates for both known (e.g., Cvt) and alternative protein sorting pathways.
proposals.method: We will isolate highly pure vacuoles from Saccharomyces cerevisiae using established, multi-step density gradient centrifugation protocols optimized in the Klionsky lab, employing mutants (e.g., pep4Δ) to prevent proteolytic degradation. Vacuolar membrane and lumenal proteins will be separated, digested, and analyzed by high-resolution tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Bioinformatic analysis will utilize MaxQuant for protein identification and quantification, followed by comparative analysis against whole-cell proteomes and databases to distinguish genuine residents from contaminants.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first definitive, high-confidence proteomic map of the yeast vacuole, providing a foundational resource for the field. It will identify new vacuolar proteins, potentially linking the organelle to unanticipated cellular functions, and will generate a candidate list for discovering novel targeting signals and pathways, building on our prior work on alternative sorting.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies focused on individual pathways or cargoes; this proposal applies a comprehensive, unbiased proteomic approach to define the entire vacuolar proteome. The systematic identification of novel residents will move the field beyond the study of known model substrates to a global understanding of vacuolar biogenesis and function.

proposals.title: Reconstitution of a Minimal Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) Pathway in Synthetic Vesicles
proposals.motivation: The yeast vacuole is a key degradative and storage organelle, and its biogenesis via pathways like the Cvt pathway is critical for autophagy and cellular homeostasis. However, the minimal machinery required for core vacuolar functions like selective cargo import remains incompletely defined. Engineering synthetic vacuoles would provide a powerful reductionist system to dissect essential components and test mechanistic hypotheses free from cellular complexity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal set of proteins, including the Cvt cargo aminopeptidase I (Ape1), its receptor Atg19, and core autophagy-related (Atg) proteins, can be reconstituted in vitro to mediate selective cargo targeting and membrane deformation in synthetic vesicles.
proposals.method: We will use a defined in vitro reconstitution approach. Giant unilamellar vesicles (GUVs) will be prepared with vacuolar membrane lipids. Purified, fluorescently tagged Ape1 propeptide (the Cvt determinant) and Atg19 will be incubated with GUVs. Recruitment of core Atg proteins (e.g., Atg8, Atg5-Atg12-Atg16 complex) will be assayed by fluorescence microscopy. Membrane remodeling and potential vesicle formation will be analyzed using time-lapse imaging and electron microscopy, comparing wild-type components to known defective mutants (e.g., in Ape1 targeting or Atg protein function).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first synthetic system for a selective vacuolar import pathway, defining the minimal protein requirements for Cvt-like vesicle formation. It will provide direct biochemical evidence for the function of key mutants and elucidate the hierarchy of protein interactions in membrane targeting. This platform will enable future dissection of other vacuolar pathways.
proposals.novelty: While in vitro reconstitution of parts of the autophagic machinery exists, building a synthetic organelle that recapitulates a specific vacuolar sorting pathway from the cytoplasm is a novel, bottom-up synthetic biology approach. It directly tests the sufficiency of known Cvt components in a minimal system, moving beyond genetic and cellular assays.

proposals.title: Cytosolic Stress Signaling as a Direct Regulator of Vacuolar Hydrolase Import via the Cvt Pathway
proposals.motivation: Research on vacuolar biogenesis, particularly the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway, has focused on how vacuolar function influences cellular states. However, the inverse regulation—how cytosolic conditions dictate vacuolar activity—remains poorly understood. This is critical for cellular adaptation, as the vacuole must integrate cytosolic signals, such as metabolic stress or protein aggregation, to modulate its degradative capacity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific cytosolic stress signals, such as nutrient limitation or proteotoxic stress, directly and acutely regulate the efficiency of cargo recognition and import via the Cvt pathway, independent of canonical transcriptional autophagy induction.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted genetic screen in S. cerevisiae using a library of cytosolic signaling mutants (e.g., PKA, TORC1, Snf1 pathways) combined with established Cvt reporters (e.g., Ape1 maturation assays). Quantitative fluorescence microscopy will track preApe1-GFP puncta formation and clearance under defined stress conditions. We will specifically use biochemical fractionation and co-immunoprecipitation to test if stress alters the interaction between cytosolic chaperones (like Hsp70/Hsp40) and the Ape1 targeting determinant, a key step in Cvt vesicle formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct signaling axis from the cytosol to the vacuolar import machinery, revealing a rapid-response mechanism for vacuolar tuning. It will identify specific cytosolic proteins that act as 'gatekeepers' for Cvt cargo, adding a new layer of regulation to the well-studied pathway and linking cellular stress perception directly to organellar function.
proposals.novelty: While the Cvt pathway's components are known, its acute regulation by cytosolic state is unexplored. This inverts the traditional perspective by positioning the cytosol not just as a source of cargo, but as an active controller of vacuolar import efficiency.

proposals.title: pH-Dependent Ligand Binding of the Vacuolar Sorting Receptor Vps10p in the Golgi Apparatus
proposals.motivation: Vacuolar protein sorting via the carboxypeptidase Y (CPY) pathway in yeast is a well-established model for understanding organelle biogenesis. While the receptor Vps10p and its ligand CPY are known, the precise molecular mechanism governing ligand binding and release in the Golgi remains unclear. Pre-2026 work suggests the acidic environment of the late Golgi and endosomes is critical for sorting, but direct biochemical evidence for pH-sensitivity of receptor-ligand interactions is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The vacuolar sorting receptor Vps10p exhibits pH-sensitive binding to its ligand CPY, with high-affinity binding at the neutral pH of the early Golgi and low-affinity or release at the acidic pH of the late Golgi/endosome, ensuring efficient cargo delivery.
proposals.method: We will employ a purified, reconstituted system using recombinant Vps10p luminal domain and proCPY. Binding affinity (Kd) will be quantitatively measured at defined pH buffers (pH 6.0 to 7.5) using surface plasmon resonance (SPR). Mutants in Vps10p (e.g., from known defective *vps* mutants) will be tested to identify residues critical for pH sensing. In parallel, we will use a *in vivo* fluorescence-based assay to monitor CPY-Vps10p colocalization and dissociation in Golgi compartments of living cells with perturbed luminal pH.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct biochemical measurement of pH-dependent binding kinetics for a vacuolar sorting receptor, establishing a mechanistic link between organelle acidification and cargo selection. It will identify specific residues in Vps10p involved in pH sensing, advancing our understanding of receptor regulation and potentially explaining sorting defects in certain mutants.
proposals.novelty: While pH is implicated in vacuolar sorting, the field lacks direct, quantitative analysis of how pH modulates the Vps10p-CPY interaction. This proposal moves beyond genetic and localization studies to a precise biochemical dissection of this key regulatory step in the Golgi.

proposals.title: Subunit-Specific Disruption of the Vacuolar ATPase Reveals Distinct Roles in Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting and Vacuolar Protein Sorting
proposals.motivation: The vacuolar ATPase (vATPase) is essential for vacuolar acidification, which is a known prerequisite for many vacuolar hydrolase activities and protein sorting pathways, including the cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway. However, pre-2026 work has primarily treated the vATPase as a monolithic complex; the specific contributions of individual subunits (e.g., the catalytic A subunit, regulatory B subunit, and proteolipid c subunit) to discrete sorting events remain poorly defined. A comparative analysis of subunit-specific defects can provide mechanistic insight into whether the vATPase influences trafficking solely through luminal acidification or via additional, subunit-specific interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disruptions to different vATPase subunits will yield quantitatively and qualitatively distinct defects in the sorting efficiency of Cvt (e.g., Ape1) and other vacuolar delivery pathways, revealing subunit-specific roles beyond bulk proton translocation.
proposals.method: We will utilize a panel of isogenic yeast strains with well-characterized, conditional or null mutations in vATPase subunits Vma1 (A), Vma2 (B), and Vma3 (c). Sorting efficiency will be quantified by monitoring the processing and maturation of the Cvt cargo aminopeptidase I (Ape1) and the vacuolar protease carboxypeptidase Y (CPY) via pulse-chase immunoprecipitation and Western blotting. We will employ fluorescence microscopy of GFP-tagged cargos and compartment markers (e.g., FM4-64) to localize sorting blocks. Genetic interactions will be tested by crossing vATPase mutants with known Cvt and vacuolar protein sorting (Vps) pathway mutants to map functional pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the hierarchy of vATPase function in vacuolar biogenesis, clarifying if all sorting defects stem from a loss of acidification or if specific subunits have direct roles in vesicle tethering or cargo recognition. It will provide a more nuanced model of how this central proton pump coordinates multiple trafficking pathways to the vacuole.
proposals.novelty: While vATPase mutants are known to be defective for vacuolar protein sorting, a systematic, side-by-side comparison of subunit-specific perturbations to dissect their mechanistic contributions to the Cvt and other pathways has not been performed, moving beyond the standard 'vATPase-null' paradigm.

proposals.title: Direct in vivo measurement of Golgi and post-Golgi vesicle pH in yeast vacuolar protein sorting (vps) mutants using pHluorin
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has established that vacuolar hydrolase sorting and organelle acidification are critical for cellular homeostasis, with defects leading to disease-like states in yeast models. While the Cytoplasm to Vacuole Targeting (Cvt) and other vacuolar protein sorting pathways are well-characterized genetically, the precise luminal pH environment of the Golgi and post-Golgi vesicles in key vps mutants remains poorly defined. This gap limits our understanding of how specific trafficking defects directly impact organelle physiology and contribute to cellular dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific classes of vps mutants, particularly those defective in late endosomal/pre-vacuolar compartment function (e.g., Class E vps mutants), will exhibit aberrant acidification profiles in the Golgi and post-Golgi vesicles, and that these pH defects correlate with the mis-sorting of vacuolar hydrolases like aminopeptidase I.
proposals.method: We will employ the pH-sensitive GFP variant, pHluorin, targeted to the lumen of the Golgi apparatus (using the GDPase signal sequence) and to post-Golgi vesicles (using a truncated form of the vacuolar alkaline phosphatase, Pho8). Using live-cell ratiometric fluorescence microscopy in wild-type and a panel of vps mutants, we will quantify luminal pH. This will be complemented by biochemical assays tracking the maturation and sorting of Ape1 (the Cvt cargo) and PrA (a CPY pathway cargo) via pulse-chase and immunoblotting to correlate trafficking defects with specific pH anomalies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, compartment-specific pH map of the secretory pathway in vps mutants, moving beyond inferences from drug treatments. It will establish a causal link between specific genetic lesions in the VPS pathway and the biophysical parameter of organelle acidity. This will refine models of how trafficking machinery regulates organelle identity and function.
proposals.novelty: While pHluorin has been used to measure cytosolic and synaptic vesicle pH, its targeted application to map the pH of distinct Golgi and post-Golgi compartments in a systematic genetic array of yeast trafficking mutants is novel. This approach directly tests the long-standing but poorly measured assumption that vps mutations alter organelle acidification.

proposals.title: In vitro reconstitution of pH-dependent cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) vesicle budding using yeast components and mammalian cell-free systems
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy in yeast, but the precise molecular mechanisms governing vesicle budding, particularly the role of luminal pH, remain incompletely defined. Pre-2026 work has established the Cvt pathway's components and genetics, yet a biochemical, cell-free assay to directly test the pH dependence of vesicle formation is lacking. Such an assay would bridge genetic and structural studies to provide mechanistic clarity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the budding of Cvt vesicles from a donor membrane is directly regulated by luminal pH, and that this pH dependence can be recapitulated in vitro using purified yeast proteins and mammalian cytosol to provide essential factors missing in yeast lysates.
proposals.method: We will establish a cell-free budding assay using purified yeast ER membranes (as a proxy donor compartment) engineered to express the Cvt cargo aminopeptidase I (Ape1) and its receptor Atg19. These membranes will be incubated with mammalian (HeLa) cytosol, an ATP-regenerating system, and purified core yeast autophagy proteins (e.g., Atg1 complex, Atg8, Atg9). Vesicle budding will be quantified by floatation centrifugation and immunoblotting for Ape1. The pH dependence will be tested by clamping luminal pH of donor membranes from 5.0 to 7.5 prior to reactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first functional in vitro reconstitution of pH-sensitive Cvt vesicle formation, directly testing a long-postulated regulatory role for the vacuolar lumen. It will identify which stage(s) of budding (cargo recruitment, coat assembly, membrane scission) are pH-sensitive. The hybrid yeast-mammalian system will provide a novel platform to dissect conserved and species-specific autophagy machinery.
proposals.novelty: While in vitro reconstitution is established for mammalian autophagy, applying this to the yeast Cvt pathway with a focus on pH is novel. The innovative use of mammalian cytosol to complement yeast lysates, based on pre-2026 knowledge of defective yeast in vitro systems, allows direct testing of biochemical mechanisms inferred from yeast mutants.

proposals.title: Genetic Suppressors of Vacuolar Aminopeptidase I Missorting Reveal Alternative Cytoplasm-to-Vacuole Targeting Pathways in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
proposals.motivation: The cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting (Cvt) pathway is a key model for selective autophagy, essential for cellular homeostasis. Mutations in core Cvt genes, like those in the ATG family, lead to defective vacuolar delivery of cargoes such as aminopeptidase I (Ape1), impairing vacuolar function and providing a model for organelle biogenesis defects. Identifying genetic suppressors of such missorting is a classic, powerful approach to uncover novel regulatory nodes and alternative trafficking routes that maintain cellular function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that suppressor mutations in non-ATG genes can restore vacuolar targeting of Ape1 in Cvt-deficient yeast strains, revealing parallel or bypass pathways for vacuolar delivery. These suppressors will identify novel genes and protein complexes that function in alternative sorting or compensate for defective core autophagy machinery.
proposals.method: We will employ a classical suppressor screen in Saccharomyces cerevisiae using a strain with a defective Cvt pathway (e.g., Δatg1 or Δatg11) expressing a chromosomally integrated Ape1-GFP reporter. Mutagenized cells will be plated and screened for colonies that restore GFP fluorescence to the vacuole lumen, indicating corrected targeting. Positive clones will be isolated, and causative suppressor mutations will be identified through whole-genome sequencing and linkage analysis via backcrossing. Candidate genes will be validated by targeted deletion and epistasis analysis with core ATG mutants.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map genetic interactions that bypass essential Cvt components, expanding the network of proteins involved in vacuolar targeting. It will contribute to understanding cellular robustness and plasticity in organelle biogenesis. The identified suppressors may represent new therapeutic targets for disorders linked to autophagy pathway dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While suppressor genetics is a standard technique, its application to systematically uncover alternative pathways for a well-defined autophagy-related cargo like Ape1 remains underexplored. This approach moves beyond characterizing known pathway components to discover entirely new genetic modules that can functionally compensate for a defective core machinery.

ideas.title: Vacuole is not the primary storage site
ideas.description: Challenge if other organelles share or dominate storage of basic amino acids and polyphosphate.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Thermal control of Vps-mediated vacuole inheritance through protein flexibility dynamics
ideas.description: This project investigates how temperature-induced changes in the structural flexibility of key proteins in the Vps sorting pathway and the vacuole inheritance machinery affect their functional interaction and the fidelity of organelle partitioning. We will employ hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) and molecular dynamics simulations across a physiological temperature range to map flexibility 'hotspots' and correlate them with functional assays of vacuole inheritance efficiency in yeast. This approach will establish a direct mechanistic link between the thermal stability of specific protein-protein or protein-membrane interfaces and the robustness of an essential cellular process, providing a new paradigm for understanding how organelle inheritance adapts to environmental stress.

ideas.title: Probing detergent-mediated membrane protein sorting kinetics in vacuole inheritance
ideas.description: This project investigates the hypothesis that detergent-like amphiphiles, such as LDAO or endogenous lipids, are not inert solubilizing agents but active modulators of the kinetics governing membrane protein sorting. We will molecularly dissect the Vps pathway during vacuole inheritance, specifically testing if these amphiphiles create a permissive chemical environment that accelerates or directs the partitioning of key transporters and pumps into inheritance machinery. This approach challenges the assumption of detergent inertness in biochemical assays and probes a potential physicochemical layer of regulation in organelle biogenesis that is overlooked by studying protein-protein interactions alone.

ideas.title: Cvt pathway is exclusive to yeast
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that Cvt is yeast-specific; similar constitutive targeting may exist in higher eukaryotes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting and autophagy employ the same machinery to deliver proteins to the yeast vacuole.

ideas.title: Transport is direct from cytosol to vacuole
ideas.description: Explore if API transits through an intermediate compartment before vacuolar delivery.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Map crosstalk between CPY and ALP pathways
ideas.description: Define the molecular mechanisms enabling the ALP pathway to deliver Vam3p for the CPY pathway.
ideas.sourceTitle: Alternative protein sorting pathways.

ideas.title: Mapping the Vps-Vacuole Inheritance Interface Using a Logistics Network Model
ideas.description: This project will molecularly dissect the intersection between the Vps protein sorting pathway and vacuole inheritance machinery by applying a conceptual framework from logistics network analysis. The analogous problem—integrating a main supply chain with a specialized, timed delivery system—is solved by mapping shared infrastructure, decision points, and resource flow rules. Adapted to cell biology, this means systematically identifying and characterizing the shared molecular components (e.g., specific Vps proteins, Rab GTPases, or phosphoinositides that serve as dual-function hubs), the regulatory checkpoints (e.g., cell-cycle kinases that modify shared machinery to switch function from homeostasis to inheritance), and the logic governing cargo routing priority at the vacuole membrane during budding. The outcome will be a dynamic, mechanistic model of how these two pathways coordinate and compete to achieve both organelle homeostasis and accurate partitioning.

ideas.title: Identify receptor for API's propeptide helix
ideas.description: Find the cytosolic or vacuolar membrane receptor that binds the essential first alpha-helix of the API propeptide.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Only Proteinase B processes API in vacuole
ideas.description: Test if other vacuolar proteases can cleave the API propeptide if Proteinase B is absent.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Determine the molecular basis of intervacuole communication by adapting principles of intercellular gap junctions
ideas.description: This research adapts the well-established solution for intercellular communication via gap junctions to the problem of intervacuole communication. In neurobiology and cell biology, gap junctions are solved by identifying specific connexin proteins that assemble into hexameric hemichannels, which dock to form regulated intercellular pores. The adapted idea is to investigate whether VAC8, VAC9, and VAC12 products mediate a similar process: forming a tethering complex at the vacuole-vacuole contact site that subsequently organizes a selective molecular channel. This would allow for the direct, regulated transfer of content or signals between the organelles, explaining how their inheritance and homeostasis are coordinated during yeast cell division.

ideas.title: Unbiased identification and mechanistic dissection of the complete molecular machinery for intervacuole communication
ideas.description: This redesigned project first uses a genome-wide genetic screen in yeast to identify all genes required for communication between mother and daughter vacuoles during cell division, moving beyond the presupposed role of VAC8/9/12. Hits from the screen are validated and ordered into a functional pathway through genetic epistasis and high-resolution live-cell imaging. The molecular basis of communication is then determined by characterizing the physical interactions, dynamic localization, and requisite activities of the core protein machinery identified in the screen, establishing a complete and validated model for this organellar communication process.

ideas.title: Vacuole inheritance via direct membrane transfer: A bacterial plasmid partitioning analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that vacuole inheritance requires vesicular carriers or de novo biogenesis by testing for direct organelle partitioning. The analogy is drawn from bacterial genetics, where the mechanism of plasmid inheritance was solved by distinguishing between de novo replication and active segregation of existing copies. The adapted research idea is to fluorescently pulse-label the vacuolar membrane, inhibit new membrane biogenesis and vesicular trafficking, and then track if the pre-labeled membrane is directly and actively partitioned into the budding daughter cell during cytokinesis, analogous to tracking pre-existing plasmids during bacterial division.

ideas.title: Investigating vacuole inheritance as a pressure-driven, self-organizing system analogous to plant cell growth
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that vacuole inheritance is solely cytoskeleton-driven by proposing it may be primarily driven by osmotic or turgor forces. Drawing an analogy from plant cell biology, where turgor pressure is the primary engine for growth and the cytoskeleton acts as a guiding regulator, we adapt this solution. The adapted idea is to investigate yeast vacuole inheritance as a pressure-driven, self-organizing system. Experiments would directly measure intravacuolar pressure and osmotic gradients during budding and test if maintaining these gradients (e.g., through osmotic manipulation) can drive vacuole movement and fission into the bud even when cytoskeletal motors (like myosin-V) are disabled. This reframes the problem: instead of the cytoskeleton 'pulling' the vacuole, it may primarily stabilize and guide a process fundamentally powered by hydrostatic forces.

ideas.title: Apply cell fractionation and proteomics
ideas.description: Isolate highly pure vacuoles and analyze their full protein composition.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Use pH-sensitive GFP to map organelle pH
ideas.description: Apply emerging pH-sensitive fluorescent proteins to directly measure Golgi and post-Golgi vesicle pH in vat mutants.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: Single-molecule FRET analysis of plant vacuole partitioning under thermal stress
ideas.description: This project applies high-temperature single-molecule FRET (from Idea A) to study the machinery responsible for plant vacuole inheritance during cytokinesis (from Idea B). The core aim is to label and monitor conformational changes in plant-specific proteins, such as VIP1 or SNAREs, as they facilitate vacuole membrane fission and partitioning in a model plant cell system under thermally stressed conditions. By doing so, it directly tests the thermal resilience and failure modes of a critical organelle inheritance process, bridging molecular biophysics with plant cell biology and stress physiology. This reveals fundamental principles of how subcellular logistics are affected by abiotic stress at the single-complex level.

ideas.title: Integrative functional and biochemical characterization of the VAC8-VAC12 module in membrane trafficking
ideas.description: This project moves beyond isolated protein biochemistry to define the functional module formed by VAC8-VAC12 gene products within the cell. We will endogenously tag each protein for live-cell imaging and purification, use BioID to map their proximal interactomes under normal and stressed conditions, and employ auxin-inducible degradation to assay acute phenotypic consequences. In parallel, purified native complexes will be subjected to detailed biochemical and structural analysis, directly coupling cellular function with molecular mechanism.

ideas.title: Define the molecular machinery's assembly and regulation
ideas.description: Elucidate how the shared machinery components assemble and are regulated differently for Cvt versus autophagy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytoplasm-to-vacuole targeting and autophagy employ the same machinery to deliver proteins to the yeast vacuole.

ideas.title: Characterize the structural dynamics and thermostability of the VAC8-VAC12 membrane motor complex
ideas.description: This project fuses the concepts by hypothesizing that the proteins encoded by the VAC8-VAC12 complementation groups form a membrane-associated motor complex essential for vacuolar function. We will biochemically characterize this complex and then specifically probe its structural dynamics using techniques like hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) and single-molecule FRET under varying temperatures. This approach directly challenges the assumption that thermostable enzymes are rigid by measuring flexibility within a native membrane motor system, potentially revealing a new mechanism where localized flexibility in key domains is critical for both motor function and thermal resilience.

ideas.title: Characterize API folding's role in transport
ideas.description: Determine how API folding state and propeptide mutations affect its recognition and import into the vacuole.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Vacuole inheritance requires direct membrane transfer
ideas.description: Test if inheritance occurs via vesicular carriers or de novo biogenesis instead of direct organelle partitioning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiple classes of yeast mutants are defective in vacuole partitioning yet target vacuole proteins correctly.

ideas.title: Study Cvt pathway in other fungi species
ideas.description: Investigate if the API targeting mechanism is conserved in other fungi or eukaryotes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Study polyphosphate storage and mobilization dynamics
ideas.description: Investigate how the vacuole regulates polyphosphate storage and release for cellular needs.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Reconstitute vacuole motility using a minimal, defined system inspired by synthetic biology
ideas.description: This idea applies the problem-solving approach from synthetic biology's minimal cell assembly to the challenge of reconstituting organelle motility. In the analogous field, complex cellular processes like gene expression are broken down and rebuilt from a defined set of purified parts to understand core principles. The adapted research idea is to reconstitute vacuole inheritance-like movement not with total cytosol, but with a minimal, defined system. Purified vacuoles would be combined in vitro with a specifically curated set of purified cytosolic factors—such as specific kinesin or dynein motors, their adaptors, regulatory proteins, and an ATP-regenerating system. This bottom-up, reductionist approach allows for precise dissection of the essential machinery and the minimal biochemical requirements for motility, enabling the testing of specific mechanistic hypotheses by adding or omitting individual components.

ideas.title: Apply Magnetic Tweezers to Characterize the Mechanochemical Coupling of the Vacuolar ATPase Fo-like Vo Sector
ideas.description: This project combines the biochemical characterization of the VAC8-VAC12 gene products, which are putative components of the yeast vacuolar ATPase's proton-translocating Vo sector, with the precise mechanical interrogation capabilities of magnetic tweezers. By purifying and reconstituting wild-type and mutant Vo complexes containing characterized VAC proteins into proteoliposomes, we can use magnetic tweezers to apply controlled torque and directly measure the force-generation (stall torque and rotational kinetics) of this Fo-like motor. This synergy allows us to assign specific mechanical functions—such as torque generation, slippage, or coupling efficiency—to individual VAC gene products, establishing a direct structure-mechanism relationship for this essential organellar rotary motor.

ideas.title: Scale up: screen for vac mutants genome-wide
ideas.description: Perform a systematic, genome-wide screen using the new assay to find all vac genes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiple classes of yeast mutants are defective in vacuole partitioning yet target vacuole proteins correctly.

ideas.title: High-Throughput 4D Analysis of Vacuole Inheritance in Controlled Microenvironments
ideas.description: This project combines advanced 3D time-lapse confocal microscopy with microfluidic cell trapping to create a high-throughput platform for studying vacuole inheritance. Single yeast cells are immobilized in precisely engineered traps, enabling their controlled growth and division over many generations while being perfused with defined media or perturbing compounds. The integrated system automatically acquires 3D volumetric data of vacuole morphology, number, and positioning at regular intervals throughout the cell cycle for entire trapped pedigrees. This allows for the systematic quantification of inheritance fidelity, the correlation of vacuole dynamics with cell cycle progression, and the direct testing of how environmental stresses or genetic perturbations affect these processes with high statistical relevance.

ideas.title: Reconstitute and Quantify Vacuole Inheritance Networks In Vitro Using FRAP
ideas.description: This project combines in vitro reconstitution with quantitative FRAP to establish a minimal system for studying vacuole inheritance. Purified vacuoles and defined cytosolic factors are used to reconstitute motility on artificial tracks, mimicking the in vivo inheritance process. FRAP is then applied within this controlled environment to simultaneously measure the kinetics of membrane and content exchange between moving vacuoles. This integrated approach allows for the direct manipulation of individual components (e.g., motor proteins, tethering factors) to determine their specific roles in coupling organelle movement with functional communication, a relationship impossible to isolate in vivo.

ideas.title: Employ multi-context synthetic genetic array analysis to map conditional and core genetic interactions of VAC8-VAC12
ideas.description: This project systematically maps the genetic interaction network of VAC8-VAC12 using SGA technology not in a single condition, but across a diverse panel of yeast genetic backgrounds and under key environmental perturbations. By performing parallel screens in strains with varied genetic makeup and under conditions like nitrogen starvation or TOR inhibition, it identifies which genetic interactions are universal ('core') and which are highly context-dependent. This multi-layered approach generates a more physiologically relevant and robust interaction map, revealing how cellular context reshapes the genetic landscape of vacuole inheritance and contact site regulation.

ideas.title: Test if propeptide can target other proteins
ideas.description: Fuse the API propeptide to heterologous cytosolic proteins to test its sufficiency as a vacuolar tag.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of a cytoplasm to vacuole targeting determinant in aminopeptidase I

ideas.title: Characterize VAC8-VAC12 gene products using structural biology and functional assays analogous to orphan receptor deorphanization
ideas.description: This research adapts the systematic deorphanization pipeline used in pharmacology for orphan GPCRs to the problem of characterizing the novel VAC8-VAC12 gene products. The analogous solution involved heterologous expression, high-throughput ligand screening, functional validation, and structural analysis. The adapted idea is to express the VAC8-VAC12 proteins, screen them against comprehensive libraries of cellular components to identify binding partners, employ functional assays relevant to vacuolar biology to determine their activities, and use structural biology techniques to elucidate their molecular mechanisms, thereby moving from unknown genes to biochemically characterized proteins with defined functions.

ideas.title: Applying Traffic Engineering Principles to Model Disrupted Vacuole Transport
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by applying an analogy from urban traffic engineering. The analogous problem is a critical intersection failure disrupting city-wide traffic flow, which is solved using network flow analysis and agent-based modeling to predict congestion and identify alternative routes. Adapted to cell biology, the study would treat the 'vac' mutant—defective in direct cytoplasm-to-vacuole (Cvt) transport—as a node failure in the vacuolar inheritance logistics network. The research would involve systematically mapping and quantifying the flow of vacuole-destined cargo through all possible alternative cellular pathways (like autophagy or endosomal systems) when the direct route is blocked. By modeling this system akin to a traffic network, the work aims to predict which cargoes are most affected, how the cell compensates, and why this specific disruption so critically impacts vacuole inheritance, thereby providing a systems-level understanding of the mutant phenotype.

ideas.title: Analyze vATPase subunit-specific sorting defects
ideas.description: Compare sorting efficiency between disruptions of different vATPase subunits (A, B, c) for mechanistic insight.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: Genome-wide screen for detergent-sensitized vacuolar ATPase motor mutants
ideas.description: This project systematically varies LDAO concentration as a kinetic perturbagen while performing a genome-wide screen for vac mutants. By quantifying motor dwell times and stepping rates across a range of LDAO conditions for each mutant, we can generate a multi-dimensional phenotypic signature for every gene knockout. This allows classification of mutants not just by whether they disrupt function, but by how they specifically alter motor kinetics and sensitivity to membrane environment, revealing genes involved in specific mechanical steps, lipid interactions, or conformational couplings within the V-ATPase complex.

ideas.title: Optogenetic tethering of vacuoles for acute, reversible mis-segregation during mitosis
ideas.description: This redesigned system employs a light-inducible heterodimerization pair (e.g., Cry2/CIB) to achieve acute and reversible vacuole mis-segregation. One component is anchored to a specific cellular structure that is not inherited at the division plane, such as the mother cell cortex or the nuclear envelope, while the other is targeted to the vacuole membrane via a specific tag. Upon blue light illumination during mitosis, vacuoles are tethered and prevented from segregating, but the tether is rapidly reversed in the dark. This allows high-resolution analysis of the immediate consequences of vacuole mis-inheritance—such as altered pH, osmotic balance, or autophagy—in the newborn daughter cell, while controlling for general phototoxicity or stress by using the same cell as its own pre-illumination control.

ideas.title: Vacuole Fusion: Testing the Synaptic Machinery Analogy
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from neuroscience, where the machinery for different vesicle fusion events (e.g., exocytosis vs. homotypic vesicle fusion) was found to share core components. The analogous solution involved identifying conserved protein families and demonstrating shared core machinery with process-specific regulators. Adapted to vacuole biology, the idea is to investigate if the machinery for intervacuole communication after inheritance is the same as for homotypic vacuole fusion. This would be tested by determining if the core SNARE complex and tethering machinery (e.g., HOPS) essential for homotypic fusion are also required for the initial communication between maternal and new vacuoles, using comparative in vitro fusion assays to dissect shared components and unique regulatory factors specific to the inheritance context.

ideas.title: Employ suppressor genetics to find bypass pathways
ideas.description: Perform genetic screens for suppressors of vat missorting, a common approach in yeast to identify alternative pathways.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: V-ATPase c-ring stoichiometry as a tunable driver of organelle mis-segregation
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the mechanistic rigidity of V-ATPase c-ring stoichiometry with active vacuole mis-segregation tools to establish a causal, biophysical link between proton pump architecture and organelle inheritance. We will engineer cells where environmental factors (like pH or ionic strength) are used not to modulate step size, but to dysregulate the activity of V-ATPases with defined c-ring stoichiometries on specific vacuoles. By then actively mis-targeting these biochemically distinct vacuoles, we can test if their fixed proton-pumping capacity directly dictates the severity of downstream cellular consequences, such as pH dysregulation or metabolic stress in the daughter cell. This allows us to treat the c-ring's stoichiometry as a tunable 'mechanical input' for precision organelle mis-segregation experiments, transforming it from a static structural feature into an active driver of inheritance phenotypes.

ideas.title: Vacuolar functions are discrete, not interrelated
ideas.description: Test if mutants can cleanly separate functions like storage and pH regulation.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Characterize sorting receptor pH sensitivity
ideas.description: Test if known vacuolar protein sorting receptors require low pH for ligand binding or release in the Golgi.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: Engineer a Synthetic 'Traffic Jam' for Vacuoles Using Programmable Molecular Adhesives
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from traffic engineering, where controlled, reversible blockades are used to create traffic jams and study systemic consequences. The analogous biological solution is to engineer inducible, synthetic protein adhesives (e.g., chemically or optogenetically dimerizable domains fused to vacuole membrane proteins). Upon induction, these adhesives will cross-link vacuoles, actively blocking their proper segregation and creating a controlled 'traffic jam' within the cell. This approach allows for the precise, temporal manipulation of vacuole mis-segregation to study the scaling consequences for cellular homeostasis, stress responses, and organelle communication networks in an inverse problem framework.

ideas.title: Employ synthetic genetic array analysis
ideas.description: Systematically map genetic interactions of VAC8-VAC12 with other cellular pathways.
ideas.sourceTitle: Multiple classes of yeast mutants are defective in vacuole partitioning yet target vacuole proteins correctly.

ideas.title: Map minimal functional sequence in first helix
ideas.description: Define the exact amino acid residues within the first alpha-helix critical for binding and targeting.

ideas.title: Microfluidic interrogation of organelle inheritance to probe environmental modulation of conserved rotary machine function
ideas.description: This project combines the high-precision environmental control and single-cell analysis of microfluidic yeast traps with a core question about the inflexibility of rotary machine function. We will engineer yeast strains where vacuole inheritance is made dependent on the function of a specific rotary ATPase (like V-ATPase) whose c-ring stoichiometry is known and genetically altered. By immobilizing populations of these cells in microfluidic devices, we can subject them to long-term, dynamically controlled environmental perturbations (e.g., pH, ionic strength, membrane tension) while quantitatively monitoring organelle inheritance fidelity. This allows us to rigorously test if environmental factors can modulate the *effective* functional output (e.g., protons translocated per ATP) of a machine whose structural step size is fixed by subunit count, thereby linking subcellular organelle dynamics to the mechanistic plasticity of molecular motors.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: cytosolic control of vacuole
ideas.description: Study how cytosolic signals and states dictate vacuolar activity, not just vice versa.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Apply in vitro reconstitution of sorting
ideas.description: Use cell-free assays from mammalian systems to test pH dependence of vesicle budding with yeast components.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: Engineer minimal synthetic vacuoles
ideas.description: Reconstitute core vacuolar functions in liposomes or synthetic vesicles.
ideas.sourceTitle: The fungal vacuole: composition, function, and biogenesis.

ideas.title: Use FRAP with a dual-compartment reporter to quantify intervacuole transport kinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic cell biology solution for measuring intercellular transport via gap junctions to the problem of quantifying intervacuole communication. In the analogous field, FRAP is used on a dye-loaded cell, where recovery after photobleaching depends on dye transfer from a connected neighbor, directly quantifying junctional permeability. Adapted here, we apply FRAP to a dual-fluorescence reporter system within yeast vacuoles. By photobleaching one vacuole labeled with separate membrane and lumenal markers, the fluorescence recovery kinetics—dependent on exchange with neighboring, unbleached vacuoles—can be used to independently measure the rates of membrane fusion (via membrane probe recovery) and content mixing (via lumenal probe recovery), providing a direct, quantitative assay for intervacuole communication dynamics.

ideas.title: Apply kinetic modeling of secretory traffic
ideas.description: Use computational modeling from cell biology to quantify kinetic delays in secretion observed in vat mutants.
ideas.sourceTitle: Compartment acidification is required for efficient sorting of proteins to the vacuole in Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

ideas.title: Establish in vitro reconstitution of API import
ideas.description: Develop a fully defined biochemical system to study API binding and translocation into vacuoles.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Trapping for Single-Organelle Inheritance Studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from single-molecule biophysics to the problem of studying organelle inheritance in immobilized cells. In biophysics, techniques like zero-mode waveguide arrays and anti-Brownian electrokinetic traps are used to confine and observe individual fluorescent molecules by actively counteracting diffusion. Adapted to yeast cell biology, this involves designing microfluidic or optofluidic devices that not only trap a single yeast cell but also apply dynamic feedback control—using gentle fluid flows, optical tweezers, or electrokinetic forces—to actively restrain a specific, fluorescently labeled vacuole within the mother cell. This 'organelle trapping' enables unprecedented, continuous observation of pre-budding localization, membrane dynamics, and inheritance events, effectively scaling down the focus from the cell to a single organelle within the immobilized system.

ideas.title: Integrate SGA with 4D vacuole phenomics to link genetic interaction networks to dynamic organelle states
ideas.description: This project fuses synthetic genetic array (SGA) analysis with automated, high-content 3D time-lapse microscopy to create a comprehensive functional map. We will perform SGA on VAC8-VAC12 query strains to generate a library of double mutants, then subject this entire library to 4D (3D + time) confocal imaging to track vacuole morphology, inheritance, and fusion/fission dynamics throughout the cell cycle. By applying advanced computer vision and machine learning for automated phenotyping, we will quantitatively link each genetic interaction score to specific, measurable defects in vacuole dynamics. This integrated approach moves beyond static interaction networks to reveal how genetic perturbations rewire the temporal and spatial control of organelle biology.

ideas.title: Environmental Modulation of ATP Synthase Step Size via Allosteric Coupling
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that the c-ring's fixed subunit count rigidly dictates the proton-to-ATP stoichiometry (step size) in ATP synthase. Drawing an analogy from enzymology, specifically the allosteric regulation of multi-subunit proteins like hemoglobin, we propose that environmental factors could modulate the effective step size. In hemoglobin, environmental modulators (pH, CO2) induce conformational changes that alter oxygen affinity without changing the number of heme groups. Adapted to ATP synthase, we will investigate whether factors such as the electrochemical gradient composition, nucleotide concentrations, or the local lipid membrane environment can act as allosteric modulators. These could change the energy required for c-ring rotation per ATP synthesized by affecting proton binding kinetics, the torsional flexibility of the ring, or its engagement with the stalk, thereby creating a variable effective stoichiometry despite a fixed c-ring architecture.

ideas.title: Genome-wide vac mutant screen using pooled CRISPR-Cas9 library approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from functional genomics/cancer biology, where pooled CRISPR-Cas9 knockout libraries are used for genome-wide screens under selective pressure. The analogous solution involves infecting a population of cells with a lentiviral library containing sgRNAs targeting every gene, applying a selection (like a drug), and using deep sequencing to quantify sgRNA abundance changes to identify essential genes. Adapted to this research context, we will perform a systematic, genome-wide screen for vac mutants by creating a pooled CRISPR-Cas9 knockout library for the organism of interest. The new vac assay will serve as the selective pressure or readout. By sequencing the sgRNA representation before and after applying the assay, we can identify all gene knockouts that result in the vac phenotype in a single, scalable experiment, moving beyond sequential or targeted gene testing.

ideas.title: Orthogonal multi-assay cascade to deconvolute direct vac gene networks
ideas.description: This redesigned approach initiates with the genome-wide screen using the new assay but immediately subjects primary hits to a stringent, integrated validation cascade using orthogonal functional assays. Candidates are sequentially tested in parallel for genetic interaction profiles and direct molecular phenotypes to filter out assay-specific artifacts and off-target effects. The resulting dataset is analyzed using network modeling to distinguish core, direct vac genes from peripheral, indirect regulators. This yields a high-confidence, functionally validated set of vac genes and maps their regulatory relationships.

ideas.title: Correlating Rotary Motor Dynamics with Inter-organelle Membrane Exchange via HS-AFM and FRAP
ideas.description: This fused approach combines high-speed atomic force microscopy (HS-AFM) to visualize the real-time rotation of a membrane protein complex, such as the V-ATPase c-ring, with Fluorescence Recovery After Photobleaching (FRAP) performed on the same membrane system to simultaneously measure the exchange of lipids or lumenal content between adjacent vacuoles or vesicles. The synergy lies in correlating the kinetic parameters of rotary motion (speed, torque, stalling) under different conditions with quantitative changes in inter-organelle communication rates. This allows the direct investigation of whether the mechanical work of a rotary pump actively modulates membrane properties to facilitate or hinder content exchange, providing a mechanistic link between molecular-scale dynamics and organelle network function.

ideas.title: Mechanistic dissection of vacuole function and inheritance using multiplexed 4D functional imaging and perturbation
ideas.description: This project combines high-resolution 4D (3D + time) lattice light-sheet microscopy with multiplexed functional readouts to move beyond descriptive morphology. Genetically encoded biosensors for luminal pH, calcium, membrane tension, and fusion/fission machinery are imaged simultaneously with structural markers to correlate dynamics with functional state. Crucially, the system integrates optogenetic actuators (e.g., vacuole-targeted CRY2/CIB for induced clustering) or rapid chemical perturbations to test causality, allowing researchers to disrupt specific vacuole processes and observe the direct consequences on organelle inheritance, cell cycle progression, and cellular homeostasis.

ideas.title: Employ a high-throughput epistasis mapping strategy using a synthetic genetic array (SGA) reporter system to map VAC8-VAC12 genetic interactions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology and network science, where high-throughput perturbation and sampling are used to map complex interaction networks like food webs. The analogous method involves stressing a key node (a species) and using environmental DNA analysis to catalog the cascading effects on the entire community. Adapted to cell biology, the solution is to employ a synthetic genetic array (SGA) analysis coupled with an automated phenotypic reporter system. By systematically creating double mutants of VAC8/VAC12 deletions with a genome-wide set of gene deletions, and then using quantitative, high-content imaging (e.g., of vacuole morphology, contact sites, or stress markers) as the readout, one can generate a massive dataset of phenotypic interactions. Network analysis and modeling of this dataset will then systematically map the genetic interactions of the VAC8-VAC12 module onto the broader landscape of cellular pathways, revealing functional connections and backup systems.

ideas.title: Dual-channel FRAP-CLEM to distinguish directed intervacuole exchange from passive diffusion
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a dual-fluorophore labeling strategy for vacuoles, combining a lumenal marker with a membrane-anchored marker. Simultaneous FRAP on both channels allows kinetic discrimination: coordinated recovery of both signals indicates directed exchange, while recovery of only the lumenal marker suggests probe leakage and cytosolic diffusion. This live-cell assay is directly coupled with correlative light-electron microscopy (CLEM) to capture the ultrastructure of any connections observed during FRAP recovery, providing definitive physical evidence for or against direct intervacuole communication pathways.

ideas.title: Probing the Catalytic Role of 'Inert' Detergents in Membrane Protein Proton Transfer
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that detergents like LDAO are kinetically inert, merely solubilizing membrane proteins without affecting reaction pathways. The analogy comes from heterogeneous catalysis, where 'inert' catalyst supports were discovered to actively participate in reactions via spillover phenomena. The analogous solution involved direct tracking of reactants across the interface using isotopic labels and tailored model systems. Adapted to this context, the research would probe the detergent's direct role in proton translocation by designing experiments to trace proton movement (e.g., with deuterium or pH-sensitive probes) specifically within the detergent micelle environment surrounding the protein, testing whether the detergent acts as a proton conduit or buffer that actively influences the kinetics of translocation.

ideas.title: Direct biophysical coupling assay of c-ring rotation to ion translocation defines mechanistic rigidity
ideas.description: This study directly tests the core mechanistic postulate that c-ring stoichiometry rigidly dictates the H+/ATP ratio. We reconstitute purified ATP synthases with c-rings of known, varying subunit counts into proteoliposomes with a controllable proton-motive force. Using single-molecule fluorescence and nano-rotation probes, we measure the real-time angular step size of the c-ring during ATP synthesis or hydrolysis and correlate it precisely with the number of ions translocated per step. This system distinguishes between a strictly rigid mechanical coupling and any environmental or allosteric modulation that could decouple the physical rotation from the bioenergetic stoichiometry, providing a definitive answer to the fundamental coupling mechanism.
