ideas.title: Adapting Enzyme Kinetics Models to Quantify LDAO's Allosteric Effect on Fo Motor Kinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the quantitative framework from enzymology and pharmacology for analyzing small-molecule effects on proteins to the study of the FoF1-ATP synthase motor. The analogous problem is determining how an inhibitor or activator precisely alters enzyme turnover, solved by measuring activity across a ligand concentration range and fitting to kinetic models. Here, LDAO detergent is treated as a modulating ligand. The adapted research involves systematically varying LDAO concentration in single-molecule or bulk assays of the Fo motor, measuring the resulting dwell times and stepping rates, and fitting the concentration-dependence data to established kinetic models. This will yield quantitative parameters (e.g., an effective KD for kinetic modulation, a Hill coefficient, or a specific effect on transition state energies) that precisely model LDAO's impact, moving beyond qualitative observation to a mechanistic, quantitative description.

ideas.title: Use cryo-EM and time-resolved mixing to capture c-ring rotational intermediates
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural enzymology, where time-resolved cryo-EM with rapid mixing and spray-freezing is used to capture short-lived enzymatic intermediates. The analogous problem of visualizing fast, transient states in reacting enzymes is solved by trapping intermediates at millisecond precision before vitrification. Adapted to the original research context, this involves applying rapid mixing techniques to ATP synthase systems, initiating proton-driven rotation by mixing proteoliposomes with an acidic buffer, and using a spray-freezing device to vitrify samples at precise time points. This approach should enable the capture of high-resolution structural snapshots of the c-ring in specific, sequential rotational stepping states that are otherwise averaged out in traditional cryo-EM preparations.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Model FRET Histograms Across Diverse c-ring Stoichiometries
ideas.description: This idea adapts the phylogenetic comparative method from evolutionary biology to the problem of extending FRET histogram analysis to motors with diverse c-ring subunit stoichiometries. In phylogenetics, a shared evolutionary model is applied across a tree of related species to understand trait variation. Analogously, we propose developing a unified physical-statistical model of the rotary cycle where the subunit number (n) is a key variable. This model, parameterized using existing FRET histogram data from motors with n=10 and n=13 subunits, would generate predicted histograms for any n (e.g., n=8, 11, 14, 15). The research would involve refining the model by testing its predictions against newly acquired experimental data for these other stoichiometries, thereby creating a general framework for interpreting single-molecule dynamics across the entire family of rotary ATPases/ATP synthases.

ideas.title: Adapting Patch-Clamp Single-Ion Channel Recording to Single-Proton Events
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from electrophysiology, where the patch-clamp technique correlates single ion channel protein gating with single-ion translocation events. The analogous solution is to isolate and measure from a single functional unit. We adapt this by creating a nano-scale experimental platform where a single proton-translocating molecular motor (e.g., F0 subunit of ATP synthase) is immobilized. A self-referencing, ultra-fast pH microsensor (like a functionalized carbon nanoelectrode) is positioned within nanometers of its proton exit port. This setup aims to detect the discrete, quantized local pH change (a 'proton blip') caused by the translocation of a single proton, directly correlating each discrete 13° rotational step, measured via a separate technique like single-molecule FRET or gold nanoparticle rotation tracking, with its causative proton transfer event.

ideas.title: Challenging the Rigidity Dogma: Measuring Local Flexibility in a Thermophilic Membrane Motor
ideas.description: This idea challenges the broad assumption that thermophilic enzymes are structurally rigid by proposing that a thermophilic membrane motor may require and possess high local flexibility for function. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where high-temperature polymers were found to have rigid bulk properties but flexible local domains, we adapt the solution of using techniques that probe dynamics at the molecular level. The adapted research will employ biophysical and computational methods (single-molecule FRET, HDX-MS, simulations) on a thermophilic membrane motor to map and measure flexibility in specific functional regions, arguing that localized, not global, dynamics are key to its high-temperature activity and stability, overturning a simplistic rigidity dogma.

ideas.description: This redesigned study systematically investigates LDAO's impact on Fo motor kinetics by rigorously controlling for non-specific membrane disruption. The approach employs defined membrane mimetics (nanodiscs) to house the Fo complex, allowing precise modulation of the local lipid environment. Kinetic measurements (dwell times, stepping rates) are performed under parallel titration with LDAO and structurally diverse detergents to differentiate class-general effects from LDAO-specific modulation. Concurrent fluorescence-based assays monitor membrane integrity and proton leakage, directly correlating bilayer perturbation with observed kinetic changes to establish causal relationships.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for 4D Vacuole Network Imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics—seismic tomography—to the problem of tracking vacuole dynamics in 4D. In seismology, waves from earthquakes are used to computationally model the Earth's interior in 3D and over time. The adapted research idea applies this principle by using controlled, sparse photobleaching or photoactivation events within the vacuole lumen as internal 'source events.' High-speed 3D confocal microscopy records the propagation of this signal. Advanced computational inversion algorithms, analogous to those in tomography, are then applied to this data set to reconstruct a high-resolution, time-lapse model of the vacuole network's morphology, connectivity, and material flow throughout the cell cycle, overcoming limitations in direct imaging depth and phototoxicity.

This multi-parameter dataset allows for the rigorous testing of whether thermal stability is governed by the preservation of essential, local flexibility while suppressing non-productive global motions, directly linking mechanistically relevant flexibility to function under thermal stress.

ideas.title: Apply Polymer Rheology Methods to Map Enzyme Flexibility-Thermostability Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from polymer rheology, where dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) maps viscoelastic transitions across temperatures, to enzymology. The analogous problem of characterizing a material's shift from glassy to rubbery states is solved by measuring storage and loss moduli under oscillatory stress during a temperature ramp. Adapted to the research context, we propose using nano-scale rheological techniques (e.g., optical tweezers-based microrheology) to apply oscillatory mechanical stress to individual enzymes across a controlled temperature range. This will measure the enzyme's temperature-dependent mechanical moduli, directly quantifying its structural flexibility landscape. The key inflection points in this flexibility profile (analogous to a polymer's Tg) will then be rigorously correlated with independent measurements of the enzyme's thermal inactivation temperature and catalytic function, establishing a causal link between motional flexibility, thermostability, and activity.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach actively controls the rotational state of the c-ring before freezing, overcoming the stochastic limitations of passive snapshot capture. Key rotational intermediates are trapped using a combination of biochemical (non-hydrolyzable ATP analogs) and biophysical (engineered disulfide crosslinks) tools to lock the c-ring relative to the stator. Each trapped state is then prepared as a structurally homogeneous sample for cryo-EM data collection. This method guarantees high particle yields for targeted stepping states, enabling reliable high-resolution reconstruction and direct mechanistic comparison of specific conformations.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from single-molecule biophysics, where the torque measurements of magnetic tweezers are validated using a directly calibrated optical trap. In studies of DNA supercoiling, an angular optical trap provides a metrology standard for torque, which is used to calibrate the more common but indirectly calibrated magnetic tweezers. For the Fo motor, we propose implementing a dual-tool setup. A precisely calibrated angular optical trap will apply known reference torques to the Fo rotor subunit. Concurrent magnetic tweezer measurements on the system will then be benchmarked against this optical standard. This adaptation solves the critical problem of absolute torque calibration in the complex Fo environment, allowing the magnetic tweezers to directly and accurately measure the force-generation (torque) of the Fo motor by first defining the relationship between applied magnetic field/rotation and true mechanical torque.

ideas.title: Stroboscopic HS-AFM with optical validation for non-perturbative imaging of rotary molecular motion
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the mechanical interference of conventional high-speed AFM by employing a synchronized, stroboscopic imaging protocol. The c-ring rotation is triggered periodically, and the AFM tip contact is limited to a brief, precisely timed window within each cycle, drastically reducing lateral drag. A parallel, non-invasive optical interferometry system continuously tracks rotational dynamics via a probe attached to the complex, providing a ground-truth validation that the motor function remains unperturbed. This hybrid system enables the correlation of direct structural snapshots from AFM with continuous functional readouts, ensuring the observed rotation is native and not an artifact of the measurement.
